confirmed.repro: Run the verify command from the previous finding and observe sym_phase1_complete=false with a 2,268,630-entry deficit; read sym_phase1_dist at kociemba_phase2_probe.cpp:1657-1668 and sym_phase1_reduced_index at 1630-1655.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat a 0xff hit as a hard error (abort with a diagnostic) in optimal-ida/two-phase proof modes, or add a one-time closure check that every visited entry's 18 children are visited; alternatively have verify-sym-phase1 assert that sym_phase1_reduced_index of a large random sample of real states never lands on 0xff.

votes.reasoning: Code reading confirms every factual claim: kociemba_phase2_probe.cpp:1663-1667 returns sym_dist_max_distance+1=13 for 0xff entries; the cached depth-12 table has 138,639,780/140,908,410 visited (deficit 2,268,630, complete=false); the byte-for-byte cross-check (lines 3813-3838) covers only raw entries with distance <=9; and the table feeds the saved bound-19 proof (native_reid_optimal_superflip_probe_seed_2026_target19.json, proves_no_solution_at_or_below_target=true) via three_axis_solved_bound. However, I substantially weakened the hazard narrative with an independent probe: recomputing the canonical (class,twist) set from phase1_sym_tables.bin in numpy and comparing per-entry against the shipped kociemba_phase1_sym_depth12.bin gives ZERO mismatches over all 140,908,410 entries — the 0xff slots are exactly the non-canonical indices, the canonical count matches exactly (138,639,780), and for all 29 stabilizer masks the canonicalization image equals its fixed-point set, so sym_phase1_reduced_index always outputs a canonical (visited) index. The fallback is provably dead code for the artifact used in the thesis proof. Additionally, the reviewer's hypothesized "build/lookup canonicalization divergence at depths 10-12" is structurally impossible: BFS build (lines 1678, 1700-1701) and lookup (line 1663) call the identical inline function in the same process. Also, "13 exceeds diameter hence inadmissible" is wrong in general — for a correctly built bounded BFS table, unvisited implies distance > 12, so max+1 is the standard admissible convention (same as full_dist at lines 2101-2102); inadmissibility requires an actual construction/cache-corruption bug. What legitimately survives: the proof-critical heuristic silently masks a would-be invariant violation instead of asserting, and load_sym_phase1_dist (lines 1722-1772) validates only magic/domain/depth with no payload integrity check, so a stale/corrupt cache would silently over-prune rather than crash. That is a real but low-impact defensive-coding gap worth one cheap assert; it cannot change any reported number given my verification. Minor is the correct severity. Repro of my verification: run /tmp/check_sym_phase1_canonical.py (parses phase1_sym_tables.bin header P1SYMR03, dtypes uint16 sym_twist/classidx, uint32 rep, uint16 stab_mask; compares canonical mask against the dist cache).

confirmed.title: solve_kociemba_native_scoped default bounds make it incomplete for arbitrary states (phase1_max_depth=10 < 12, phase2_max_depth=14 < 18)
confirmed.claim: The native scoped two-phase solver's defaults cannot solve all cube states even with unlimited time: phase-1 needs up to depth 12 and phase-2 up to 18, but the defaults cap at 10 and 14 (plus 2M-node and 10 s budgets). On random depth-16/20 scrambles it returns status lower_bound or timeout. The thesis only claims the fixed 13-case corpus (all verified non_exact — true), but a supervisor asking 'can your Kociemba solve any state?' will hit this; the failure notes say 'lower bound exceeds configured max depth' which is honest but easy to misread as a proof statement.
confirmed.evidence: Probe over 12 random scrambles with timeout_seconds=20: trials at k=16/20 produced {'status': 'lower_bound', 'length': None} (3 of 12), e.g. trial 1 (k=16) and trials 6/10 (k=20); all 9 successes verified with correct G1 handoff and legal phase-2 moves. Defaults at kociemba.py:692-699: phase1_max_depth=10, phase2_max_depth=14.
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=src /opt/miniconda3/bin/python3 /tmp-style script: scramble 16-20 random non-same-face moves, call solve_kociemba_native_scoped(cube, timeout_seconds=20) and observe lower_bound failures; compare with phase-1 diameter 12 / phase-2 diameter 18.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Raise defaults to phase1_max_depth=12 and phase2_max_depth=18 (completeness in principle, budgets still bound runtime), or state explicitly in the thesis implementation chapter that the scoped path is depth-bounded below the subgroup diameters by configuration.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by code re-read and probes. Defaults at src/rubik_optimal/solvers/kociemba.py:695-696 are phase1_max_depth=10, phase2_max_depth=14. The phase-1 collector is hard depth-capped regardless of time (range(initial_lower_bound, max_depth+1) at line 540; g >= max_depth cutoff at line 513; root guard 473-476), so states at phase-1 distance 11-12 (diameter is 12) provably yield zero handoff candidates and the solver returns status lower_bound with no solution (lines 728-747) even with unlimited time. Phase-2 is capped at 14 vs diameter 18 (lines 602-606, 633, 664). All production call sites (benchmark.py:127-133, cli.py:922-927, end_to_end.py) use the same 10/14 caps. Empirically, 8/8 random depth-16/20 scrambles failed at default settings (10 s timeout), statuses timeout/lower_bound, all honest (is_verified=False, no false exactness). One caveat correcting the reviewer's evidence: their lower_bound trials are not clean witnesses of unlimited-time incompleteness — re-running my trial-1 scramble (R2 F2 U2 L F2 L' R B2 F' U2 L' R' U' F2 R F2 R' F') with the same depth caps but timeout=50 s solved it (verified length 20, non_exact), showing the aggregate lower_bound status can be time-driven (it propagates best_phase2_for_diag.status, line 869, even when later candidates were never tried). The incompleteness claim stands on the airtight diameter argument, not their specific trials. Severity minor is right: the thesis claims only the 13-case corpus for this solver (05_experiments.tex:397), explicitly disclaims universal solving (01_introduction.tex:45, c_submission_checklist.tex:29-31), and all failure statuses are honest, so no reported number or claim is wrong — it is a live-demo/limitations risk, not a soundness defect.

confirmed.title: Phase-2 encoders do not validate orientations, so a non-G1 state can be silently accepted by solve_kociemba_phase2
confirmed.claim: The three phase-2 coordinates check only edge positions (slice edges in slice positions), never CO/EO. A cube with all edges positioned in their home slice but nonzero corner/edge orientations encodes without raising, so solve_kociemba_phase2 (kociemba.py:573) would run and could return an IDA* result labeled 'exact' whose move sequence does NOT solve the real cube. All current callers are guarded (handoffs come from a verified phase-1 coordinate goal, and both solvers replay-verify the final solution before claiming anything), so there is no live bug — but the function contract relies entirely on a docstring.
confirmed.evidence: encode_phase2_corner_permutation is just 'return rank_permutation(cube.cp)'; the native equivalent (kociemba_phase2_probe.cpp:411-423, encode_phase2) DOES throw on nonzero co/eo, so the Python and C++ contracts diverge. The error path in solve_kociemba_phase2 (kociemba.py:585-591) only catches edge-position ValueErrors.
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=src python3 -c "from rubik_optimal.cube import CubeState; from rubik_optimal.solvers.kociemba import solve_kociemba_phase2; c=CubeState.solved().apply_sequence(['F']); c2=c.apply_sequence(['F','F','F']);  # instead: construct a state with co=(1,2,0,...) eo solved, ep identity, and observe encode succeeds" — concretely: CubeState(cp=tuple(range(8)), co=(1,2,0,0,0,0,0,0), ep=tuple(range(12)), eo=(0,)*12) passes all three spec.encode calls despite not being in G1.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Mirror the native check: raise ValueError in the phase-2 encode functions when any co/eo component is nonzero, so the existing except-ValueError path in solve_kociemba_phase2 reports 'outside the phase-2 subgroup'.

votes.reasoning: Verified by reading the code and running the repro. (1) phase2.py:28-35 validates only edge positions and phase2.py:38-39 is bare rank_permutation(cube.cp); no encoder checks co/eo. (2) Executed repro: CubeState(cp=identity, co=(1,2,0,...), ep=identity, eo=0) — a valid cube outside G1 — encodes to the goal triple (0,0,0) and solve_kociemba_phase2 returns status='exact' with an EMPTY solution ('goal already satisfied') for an unsolved cube, which is even stronger than the reviewer's phrasing. (3) Native kociemba_phase2_probe.cpp encode_phase2 (~lines 411-423) does throw on nonzero co/eo, confirming the Python/C++ contract divergence. (4) kociemba.py:585-591 only catches the edge-position ValueError. (5) The 'no live bug' mitigation is also accurate: both production callers replay-verify with verify_solution before claiming anything (kociemba.py:826; kociemba_optimal.py:279), the phase-1 handoff enumeration in kociemba_optimal.py only accepts states whose three phase-1 pruning values are all 0 at g==depth (hence genuine G1), and the two test callers scramble only with phase-2-subgroup moves. Severity 'minor' is correct: a latent defense-in-depth gap with a demonstrable false-'exact' failure mode if any future caller skips verification, but no currently reachable unsound result. Fix is a one-line co/eo check mirroring the native probe.

confirmed.title: Disk-cache trust chain: unmanifested table files are adopted on structural checks only, bypassing the checksum gate that protects the optimality-claiming solver
confirmed.claim: _load_cached_rows trusts a cached move/pruning table whose file is absent from the manifest after checking only name/kind/move-order/row-count — values are never re-derived or spot-checked. solve_kociemba_two_phase_optimal's status='exact' proof depends on these cached phase-1/phase-2 pruning values being admissible: a corrupted-but-well-formed JSON (e.g. inflated pruning entries) placed in data/generated without a manifest entry would be adopted as ground truth and could prune the true optimal maneuver, yielding a false 'exact'. The current cache contents are correct (I validated all six tables against the cube model), so this is a hardening gap, not a live defect.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 173-177: 'When the manifest had no entry for this file (e.g. pre-existing artifacts), adopt the validated file's checksum so subsequent loads are checksum-gated.' — adoption happens with no value-level validation. Current tables verified clean: 300 random coords x all moves per table matched spec.encode(decode(c).apply_move(m)) with 0 mismatches; pruning tables 1-Lipschitz, solved=0, no -1 entries.
confirmed.repro: Read kociemba.py:128-177; then run the table-validation probe (sample coords from _phase1_coordinate_tables()/_phase2_coordinate_tables() and compare each move-table entry against direct cube-model recomputation) to confirm today's cache is clean.
confirmed.suggested_fix: On adoption (manifest miss), spot-check N random move-table rows against direct recomputation and verify pruning-table 1-Lipschitz consistency before trusting the file, or refuse adoption in contexts that claim exactness (kociemba_optimal could pass a strict=True flag).

votes.reasoning: CONFIRMED by independent code re-read and empirical probe. The cited path is exactly as described: in /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/solvers/kociemba.py, _load_cached_rows applies the SHA-256 gate only when the manifest already has an entry (line 152: `if expected_digest is not None and digest != expected_digest`). For an unmanifested file, only structural checks run (schema_version, table_name, table_kind, moves tuple, rows-is-list, len==domain_size; lines 159-171) — no value-level validation — and lines 175-176 adopt the file's own digest into the manifest. I verified this empirically: with RUBIK_OPTIMAL_DATA_DIR pointed at a copy of the real tables minus the manifest, I inflated one corner-orientation pruning entry (5 -> 19) in a well-formed JSON; _phase1_coordinate_tables() served the tampered value AND wrote a manifest blessing the tampered file, so all subsequent loads pass the checksum gate while serving inadmissible data (self-blessing makes the corruption persistent). The downstream dependency also checks out: solve_kociemba_two_phase_optimal (/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/solvers/kociemba_optimal.py line 182) consumes these tables; inflated pruning values would raise total_floor (lines 205-206) and cut phase-1 enumeration branches (lines 121, 132), and verify_solution (line 279) only proves the returned sequence solves the cube, not that it is optimal — so a false status='exact' on a suboptimal solution is reachable. The docstring at kociemba.py lines 135-142 ("This guarantees the loaded numbers are the same deterministic table the solver would have computed in-memory") is factually false on the adoption path. Severity 'minor' is right: (1) the manifest currently exists and covers all 12 table files, so the adoption path is dormant for actual thesis runs; (2) I independently rebuilt all six tables in-memory and diffed against the repo cache — exact match, so there is no live data defect and no reported number is affected; (3) triggering it requires an out-of-band actor placing a well-formed wrong table without a manifest entry (or deleting the manifest, which makes ALL files adoption-eligible — a slightly broader surface than the finding states); (4) the scheme is inherently self-referential anyway (the manifest lives in the same writable directory as the tables), so even the full checksum gate never defended against deliberate tampering — the marginal protection lost is small. One quibble: the finding cites lines 171-177 but the gate-skip starts at line 152; the substance is unaffected. Recommended fix for the student: spot-check a few rows against direct recomputation (spec.encode(decode(c).apply_move(m)) and pruning consistency) before adopting an unmanifested file, or simply regenerate instead of adopting, and correct the overstated docstring.

confirmed.title: Scoped Korf solver underreports table_bytes/edge_pdb_bytes relative to the tables its heuristic actually uses
confirmed.claim: solve_korf_ida reports table_bytes = coordinate_pruning_table_bytes() + corner_pdb_bytes + edge_pdb_bytes, where edge_pattern_database_bytes() counts only the eight 6-edge PDBs. Its heuristic (combined_table_lower_bound -> heuristic_lower_bound_components) now also consults the two 7-edge PDBs (1,021,870,224 bytes) when present, so the reported memory figure and the 'edge_pdb_bytes=' note understate the tables consulted by about 1.02 GB on this tree. Any new benchmark row would pair changed node counts with an unchanged table-bytes figure, which is internally inconsistent.
confirmed.evidence: korf.py:62-63 computes edge_pdb_bytes from edge_pattern_database_bytes() (heuristics.py:193-196, which sums default_edge_pdb_paths() only), while heuristics.py:116 adds the edge_pdb7 component to the same heuristic; seven_edge_pattern_database_bytes() exists (heuristics.py:162-165) but is never added in korf.py.
confirmed.repro: Read korf.py:61-76 against heuristics.py:108-117 and 193-196; confirm data/generated contains both 7-edge bins so seven_edge_pattern_database_lower_bound returns nonzero (my probe: edge_pdb7 component reached 10 on random scrambles).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Include seven_edge_pattern_database_bytes() in table_bytes (and in the notes string) whenever the edge_pdb7 component is active, or gate the component off by default per the previous finding.

votes.reasoning: Independent re-read confirms every link in the chain. korf.py:61-63 builds table_bytes from coordinate_pruning_table_bytes() + corner PDB + edge_pattern_database_bytes(), and heuristics.py:193-196 shows edge_pattern_database_bytes() sums only default_edge_pdb_paths() (the eight 6-edge PDBs). Meanwhile korf.py:69 uses combined_table_lower_bound, whose component dict (heuristics.py:108-117) unconditionally includes 'edge_pdb7': seven_edge_pattern_database_lower_bound(cube) at line 116. Both 7-edge binaries exist in data/generated (510,935,112 bytes each, total 1,021,870,224 = the claimed 1.02 GB), and a runtime probe confirmed edge_pdbs_7_available()==True with a nonzero edge_pdb7 component (8 on an 8-move scramble). seven_edge_pattern_database_bytes() (heuristics.py:162-165) is never imported into korf.py. The codebase convention elsewhere contradicts korf.py: optimal_native.py:304 correctly adds edge_pdb_7_size_bytes to its table accounting. One nuance the original finding glossed over: the saved benchmark rows (benchmarks_seed_2026_thesis.csv, mtime Jun 8 18:30) predate the 7-edge binaries (Jun 9 00:02/00:30), so existing saved data is internally consistent; the inconsistency is prospective — any re-run or new benchmark now produces changed node counts paired with an unchanged table_size_bytes=428,808,562 and a misleading 'edge_pdb_bytes=' note understating consulted tables by ~1.02 GB, and the saved node counts become silently non-reproducible. No correctness, admissibility, or optimality impact, and the thesis text does not cite the byte figure directly, so 'minor' is the right severity. Fix is a one-line addition of seven_edge_pattern_database_bytes() to table_bytes (and ideally the note) in korf.py.

confirmed.title: Stale '~13' max-distance claims for 7-edge PDBs contradict the measured maximum of 11
confirmed.claim: Comments/docstrings claim the 7-edge projection 'reaches deeper (max distance ~13 vs the 6-edge PDB's 10)'. Both generated 7-edge PDBs actually max at distance 11, as recorded in their own metadata (results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_seed_2026_thesis.json: max_distance 11) and corrected in docs/limitations.md:31 and the WORSTCASE doc's header (line 20: 'maxes at distance 11 (not the ~13 estimated in 3)'). The pre-measurement estimate was never removed from the code comments or from WORSTCASE line 541, so the repo contradicts its own measured artifacts.
confirmed.evidence: My histogram probe of thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_pdb.bin: max=11, hist={...,10: 10455452, 11: 22}; thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_5_6_7_8_9_10_11_pdb.bin: max=11, {11: 18}. edge_pdb.py:75 reads '(max distance ~13 vs the 6-edge PDB's 10)'.
confirmed.repro: python3 - then numpy.fromfile the 7-edge bin at offset 72 and bincount: max nonzero index is 11; grep -n '~13' src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md
confirmed.suggested_fix: Update the three '~13' comments to the measured 11 (and note that 11 is realized only on permutation-scrambled projections, per limitations.md).

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by independent re-read and re-measurement. (1) The stale text exists exactly as cited: /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py:73-76 says the 7-edge projection "reaches deeper (max distance ~13 vs the 6-edge PDB's 10)", and /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py:145-146 (docstring of seven_edge_pattern_database_lower_bound) repeats "max distance ~13 vs 10". (2) The measured truth is 11: both metadata artifacts (results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_seed_2026_thesis.json and ..._subset_5_6_7_8_9_10_11_...) record max_distance=11, and my own numpy histogram of the two 511 MB binaries (offset 72, matching the HEADER layout verified in edge_pdb.py:228-281) reproduces the reviewer's counts exactly: max value 11, tails {10: 10455452, 11: 22} and {10: 11847292, 11: 18}. (3) The repo corrects itself in docs/limitations.md:31 ("maxes at distance 11 (not the doc's hoped ~13)") and in WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md:13-20 (the 2026-06-09 UPDATE explicitly says the measured outcomes "revise the optimistic estimates below" and names line 541's section, sec 3), so the code comments do contradict the repo's own measured artifacts. One nuance the report should keep: WORSTCASE line 541 sits inside sec 3, the pre-measurement design section that the doc's header explicitly supersedes, so that instance is arguably acceptable as a labeled historical estimate; the genuinely stale instances are the two unconditional present-tense code comments/docstrings. Mitigating facts that cap severity: "~13" appears nowhere in executable logic (no hardcoded bound; the heuristic reads measured table values), nowhere in thesis/chapters/, and the heuristic's admissibility/behavior is unaffected. Repro: grep -n '~13' src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md; then python3 numpy.fromfile on data/generated/thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_pdb.bin with offset 72 and bincount (max nonzero index is 11). Fix is a two-line comment edit. Severity "minor" is correct: documentation-only inconsistency, no effect on results or claims of optimality.

confirmed.title: Additive (cost-partitioned) PDB compatibility is not machine-checkable: binary header stores no move-cost vector
confirmed.claim: The admissibility of summing --additive-edge-pdb tables depends on their 0/1 move-cost vectors partitioning the 18 moves, but the on-disk header records only the R3ECPD1 magic, not the cost vector (it lives only in the side metadata JSON). The native loader correctly requires the additive magic and complete=1, but it cannot detect incompatible combinations: passing the same urf cpdb twice (or two cpdbs whose costed faces overlap) would be summed into an inadmissible bound with no error. Same for Python's additive_edge_cpdb_lower_bound, which sums whatever paths it is given and does not even check header.cost_partitioned. The shipped default pair (urf U/R/F + dlb D/L/B) is a valid partition and I verified its sum is admissible (zero violations over 2,300 probes; zero-cost layers match independent free-move orbit BFS: 48,384 and 4,032 states), so current artifacts and default call sites are sound; this is a robustness hole for non-default invocations behind an exactness claim.
confirmed.evidence: Header struct in edge_pdb.cpp:59-76 has no move_costs field; optimal_solver.cpp:1318-1331 validates magic/dimensions/complete only; heuristics.py:168-178 sums distances from load_edge_pdb(path) with no cost_partitioned check (EdgePatternDatabase accepts both magics, edge_pdb.py:252).
confirmed.repro: Read the three cited ranges; to demonstrate: run native/build/optimal_solver with '--additive-edge-pdb <urf.bin> --additive-edge-pdb <urf.bin>' on a scrambled state and observe initial_lower_bound doubles versus a single copy (do not use the result).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Write the 18-byte move-cost vector into the header (there is room: bump header_bytes or a v2 magic) and have loaders verify that the supplied additive set's costs sum to exactly 1 per move; cheaper stopgap: reject duplicate paths and document the partition requirement in --help.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by independent code re-read and a live repro. (1) The on-disk header (native/edge_pdb/edge_pdb.cpp:59-77, packed 72 bytes) stores no move-cost vector; the 18-entry move_costs (line 90) only flips the magic to R3ECPD1 when non-uniform (lines 520-523) and is written solely to the side metadata JSON (lines 558-565). (2) The native loader (native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp:1312-1355) validates magic/version/dimensions/complete only; CLI parsing (2204-2205) and loading (2439-2441) accept arbitrary path multisets, and forward_heuristic (1367-1379, also 1578-1585) sums all loaded additive PDBs unconditionally. (3) Python additive_edge_cpdb_lower_bound (src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py:168-178) sums distances with no cost_partitioned check; additive_edge_pdbs_available (edge_pdb.py:372-374) checks only existence and load_edge_pdb accepts both magics (edge_pdb.py:252). Demonstration: on state U R F (true optimal distance 3, urf cpdb value 2), passing thesis_seed_2026_edge_cpdb_urf_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5.bin twice via --additive-edge-pdb loads with no error and yields initial_lower_bound 4 (> true distance 3, inadmissible) while still reporting status "exact"; the valid urf+dlb pair stays admissible. One minor wording correction: the Python function takes no paths argument — it is hard-wired to the default urf/dlb spec pair (edge_pdb.py:120-131), so Python exposure requires a mis-generated file at a default path (which the missing in-binary cost vector makes undetectable). Defaults and shipped artifacts are sound, so this is a robustness/validation hole for non-default invocations, not a defect in any reported number — severity "minor" is correct.

confirmed.title: generate_edge_pdb.py reuse check cannot distinguish different cost vectors for the same output
confirmed.claim: The skip-regeneration condition compares only bool(existing.get('cost_partitioned')) with bool(requested costs contain a 0). If a cost-partitioned table at the same output path already exists, re-running with a different non-uniform --move-costs vector silently reuses the stale table (and its metadata), labeling old contents with the script's exit status. With the default AdditiveEdgePDBSpec set the file name encodes the spec label so collisions are unlikely, but the --subset + --move-costs CLI path writes to the cost-agnostic thesis_seed_*_edge_subset_<label>_pdb.bin name, where a unit-cost table and a custom 0/1-cost table share a path.
confirmed.evidence: Line 157: 'if existing.get("complete") is True and max_depth is None and bool(existing.get("cost_partitioned")) == bool(table_move_costs and any(cost == 0 for cost in table_move_costs)):' - no comparison of existing.get('move_costs') against table_move_costs; line 147 shows the non-additive output path does not encode costs.
confirmed.repro: Read scripts/generate_edge_pdb.py:144-160. Dry-reason: two invocations with --subset 0,1,2,3,4,5 and different --move-costs vectors map to the same output path and the second is skipped if metadata says complete.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Compare existing.get('move_costs') == list(table_move_costs) in the reuse condition, and/or encode a cost-vector tag in the non-additive output filename when costs are non-uniform.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by both code re-read and a dynamic repro. scripts/generate_edge_pdb.py:157 gates skip-regeneration on bool(existing.get("cost_partitioned")) == bool(requested vector contains a 0) and never compares existing.get("move_costs") to the requested table_move_costs; line 147 builds the non-additive output path from the subset label only, so every cost vector for a subset maps to the same .bin/.json. Since _parse_move_costs restricts costs to {0,1}, the boolean check catches unit-cost vs zero-containing mismatches, but two DIFFERENT zero-containing vectors are indistinguishable. Dynamic proof (all in /tmp, repo untouched): planted a fake complete metadata with move_costs [0,0,0,1x15] and a garbage bin at /tmp/epdb_probe/data/generated/quick_seed_99_edge_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_pdb.bin, then ran the script with --subset 0,1,2,3,4,5 --move-costs 1x15,0,0,0: it exited 0 without regenerating, left the stale bin bytes intact, and emitted a summary whose subsets entry carries the OLD move_costs vector rather than the requested one. The native generator (native/edge_pdb/edge_pdb.cpp:558) confirms cost_partitioned just means non-uniform cost, matching the Python bool. Impact is contained: all saved non-additive thesis metadata (results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_subset_*.json) are uniform unit-cost, and the thesis additive pipeline writes to spec-label-encoded paths (src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py:155-167), so no existing thesis artifact was produced through the colliding path; the bug requires two manual CLI runs with distinct zero-containing vectors on the same subset. Latent tooling footgun (could silently hand back a wrong cost-partitioned table, which if later summed additively would be unsound), but it does not affect any reported number, so 'minor' is the correct severity.

confirmed.title: New docstrings claim 7-edge PDB max distance "~13" but the generated tables measure 11
confirmed.claim: The just-added docstring of seven_edge_pattern_database_lower_bound says "max distance ~13 vs 10", and the DEFAULT_EDGE_SUBSETS_7 comment in edge_pdb.py repeats "max distance ~13 vs the 6-edge PDB's 10". The repo's own measurements (and docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md section 0, which explicitly corrects the section-3 estimate) show the complete 7-edge PDB maxes at 11. The code comments contradict the saved evidence the thesis relies on.
confirmed.evidence: Histogram over both complete 510,935,040-entry tables: real_max=11, unvisited=0 (probe output: "thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_pdb.bin: ... hdr_max=11 real_max=11 unvisited=0"); results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_seed_2026_thesis.json records max_distance=11; WORSTCASE doc section 0: "maxes at distance 11 (not the ~13 estimated in section 3)".
confirmed.repro: grep -n '~13' src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py; python3 -c "import json; print(json.load(open('results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_seed_2026_thesis.json'))['max_distance'])"
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change both comments to the measured value 11 (citing seven_edge_strength_seed_2026.json / the metadata JSONs), keeping the honest framing that the gain over 6-edge is +1 max and +0 on the superflip.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct reading and data checks. (1) src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py:145-146 docstring asserts the 7-edge PDB "max distance ~13 vs 10", and src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py:73-76 repeats "max distance ~13 vs the 6-edge PDB's 10"; neither hedges it as a superseded estimate. (2) Both complete 7-edge PDB metadata files (results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_seed_2026_thesis.json and subset_5_6_7_8_9_10_11) record max_distance=11 with complete:true, and I verified each distance histogram sums to exactly 510,935,040 = C(12,7)*7!*2^7, so 11 is the exhaustively measured max (only 22/18 states at depth 11, none deeper). (3) The repo already self-corrected: docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md:20 ("maxes at distance 11, not the ~13 estimated in §3") and docs/limitations.md:31 say the same; only the two code comments retain the stale estimate. Refutation attempts failed: the "~13" in the comments is unambiguously a PDB max-distance claim (distinct from the ~13.3 branching-factor figure elsewhere), the tables are provably complete, and the comments are phrased as fact. Severity "minor" is correct: documentation-only inconsistency; the heuristic reads exact table distances so admissibility and all saved/thesis numbers are unaffected (no "~13" appears in thesis/chapters/*.tex), and the qualitative claim (7-edge reaches deeper than 6-edge, 11>10) remains true. Fix: change "~13" to the measured "11" in both comments.

confirmed.title: kociemba_phase2_lower_bound guard is inconsistent: rejects non-G1 permutations but silently accepts orientation-unsolved states
confirmed.claim: Calling kociemba_phase2_lower_bound on a state whose UD/slice edges are out of position raises ValueError, but a state with G1-like permutation and unsolved orientations (e.g. the superflip, eo all 1) silently returns a finite "phase-2 lower bound" (3 for the superflip) even though no phase-2 solution exists for it. The value is vacuously sound as a lower bound, but the asymmetric guard makes the exposed function easy to misuse outside its intended phase-2-restricted context. It is currently only exercised by tests/test_coordinates_and_tables.py:108, never by a production solver (solvers/kociemba.py uses its own kociemba_phase2_projection_lower_bound).
confirmed.evidence: Probe output: random non-G1 states raise "ValueError: Phase-2 edge projection expected edges (0..7) in positions (0..7), got (8, 10, 7, 0, 4, 9, 11, 3)", while kociemba_phase2_lower_bound(superflip) == 3 returns without error. _rank_projected_edges (coordinates/phase2.py:31) validates only edge positions, never cube.eo/cube.co.
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=src python3 -c "from rubik_optimal.cube import CubeState; from rubik_optimal.search.heuristics import kociemba_phase2_lower_bound as f; sf=CubeState(cp=tuple(range(8)),co=(0,)*8,ep=tuple(range(12)),eo=(1,)*12); print(f(sf)); print(f(CubeState.from_sequence('F')))"
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either also reject states with nonzero eo/co (full G1 membership check) or document in the docstring that the value is meaningful only for true G1 states searched under PHASE2_MOVES.

votes.reasoning: Verified by independent code read and live probe. (1) src/rubik_optimal/search/heuristics.py:81-88 computes max(misplaced_cubie_lower_bound, phase-2 projection pruning values) with no orientation check; the only validation on the path is _rank_projected_edges at src/rubik_optimal/coordinates/phase2.py:28-35, which checks edge piece/position sets only and never reads cube.eo or cube.co (the corner projection at phase2.py:38-39 has no guard at all). (2) Probe reproduces the asymmetry exactly: the superflip (identity permutations, eo all 1) returns 3 silently — vacuous since phase-2 moves preserve edge orientation so no phase-2 solution exists for it — while a state after one F move raises ValueError from phase2.py:31. (3) Usage claims confirmed by grep: the production Kociemba solver (src/rubik_optimal/solvers/kociemba.py:303,757) uses its own kociemba_phase2_projection_lower_bound; the heuristics.py function is exercised only by tests/test_coordinates_and_tables.py:108 with genuine phase-2 sequences, so no reported result is affected. The finding is real but correctly scoped as an API-hygiene/misuse-risk issue: the guard is incidental (ranking fails without it) rather than a deliberate domain check, and the orientation precondition is documented only in phase2.py's module docstring, not enforced or documented on the exposed heuristic. Severity 'minor' is appropriate since nothing in the thesis or solvers depends on the function outside its valid domain.

confirmed.title: Committed Python IDA* optimality regression only certifies distances <= 4 (14 of 21 corpus cases skip)
confirmed.claim: _OPTIMALITY_BFS_DEPTH = 4 caps the BFS oracle, so test_korf_and_ida_match_bfs_exact_distance skips every corpus scramble whose true distance exceeds 4 (14 of 21 cases skip in the current run). The skips are honest (never silently passed), but the committed gate for the Python IDA*/combined-heuristic path certifies optimality only on the distance<=4 ball, which is thin for the thesis's central optimality property.
confirmed.evidence: pytest -rs output: "SKIPPED [1] tests/test_search_and_solvers.py:238: optimal distance of (...) exceeds BFS oracle depth 4" x14; 60 passed, 14 skipped. My independent probe (21 random states, scramble length 1-7, native solver as oracle) found zero optimality failures, so this is a coverage gap, not a latent bug.
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/test_search_and_solvers.py -q -rs | tail -20
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a native-marked variant that uses solve_korf_native_optimal as the oracle for scramble lengths 5-8 (skipping when the binary/PDBs are absent), mirroring the probe that already passes.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code re-read and a live pytest run. tests/test_search_and_solvers.py line 89 hard-caps the BFS oracle at depth 4; test_korf_and_ida_match_bfs_exact_distance (lines 219-257) skips unconditionally at lines 237-242 whenever the oracle returns None. Running `PYTHONPATH=src python3 -m pytest tests/test_search_and_solvers.py -q -rs` reproduces exactly 14 skips, all at line 238 with the "exceeds BFS oracle depth 4" message (60 passed, 14 skipped). One correction to the finding's numbers: the corpus is 30 parametrized cases (lines 92-101, range(30)), not 21, so the split is 16 run / 14 skip. Grep across tests/ confirms no other test certifies the Python solve_korf_ida / ida_star_solve + combined_table_lower_bound path at distances > 4 (other references are monkeypatched mocks; admissibility checks in test_seven_edge_heuristic.py are also BFS-capped at depth 5), so the coverage gap is real and unmitigated for the Python path. The skips are honest and documented in the code comment (lines 80-88), the reviewer's probe found no actual optimality failures, and deeper optimality coverage exists for the separate native solver path that carries the thesis benchmarks — so this is a test-coverage thinness issue, not an unsound result. Severity "minor" is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Node-limit termination is reported with status "timeout"
confirmed.claim: When the node budget (not the wall clock) is exhausted, ida_star_solve and ida_star_collect_solutions return status="timeout" with a distinguishing note ("IDA* node limit reached"). Downstream distance.py maps this to kind="unknown_timeout", so no exactness over-claim occurs, but the status enum conflates two different budgets, and any thesis table keyed on status alone would mislabel node-limited runs as wall-clock timeouts.
confirmed.evidence: ida_star.py:179-183: `if node_budget_exceeded(): return IDAStarResult(None, expanded, generated, max_depth, "timeout", runtime, lower_bound, "IDA* node limit reached")` - the status literal is "timeout" while the note says node limit.
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=src python3 -c "from rubik_optimal.cube import CubeState; from rubik_optimal.search.ida_star import ida_star_solve; r=ida_star_solve(CubeState.from_sequence('R U F D L B'), max_depth=8, timeout_seconds=60, node_limit=100); print(r.status, '|', r.notes)"
confirmed.suggested_fix: Introduce a distinct "node_limit" status (and map it alongside timeout in distance.py), or at minimum document the conflation where the statuses are consumed.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code reading and execution. ida_star.py:179-183 returns status="timeout" with note "IDA* node limit reached" when only the node budget is exhausted (the wall-clock check at line 174 is evaluated first and was false); ida_star_collect_solutions does the same at lines 312-314. The repro (node_limit=100, timeout_seconds=60) returned status "timeout" after 102 nodes with virtually zero elapsed time, proving the node budget — not the clock — triggered the status. Downstream, distance.py:129-132 maps status=="timeout" to kind="unknown_timeout", so there is no exactness over-claim, matching the reviewer's account. Mitigating context: the project-wide status enum (benchmark.py:444, thesis_audit.py:129) has no node-limit member, so the conflation appears to be a deliberate convention with the notes field disambiguating, and no saved result file in results/processed/ contains "node limit reached", so no thesis number is currently affected. It remains a real labeling defect: any table or aggregation keyed on status alone would mislabel node-limited runs as wall-clock timeouts. Minor is the correct severity.

confirmed.title: WORSTCASE design doc describes the root-stabilizer prune as 24 proper rotations with 3 superflip representatives; the code now uses the full 48-element group with 2 representatives
confirmed.claim: Section 0.1 states symmetry.py "computes the 24 proper whole-cube rotations that stabilize the input state" and that the superflip mask reduces first moves "from 18 to 3 (U, U', U2)" with root_move_count=3. The current implementation (src/rubik_optimal/symmetry.py:297-320 docstring: "full 48-element whole-cube symmetry group, including ... reflections") produces 2 representatives (U, U2), as asserted by tests/test_optimal_native.py:223 and by the saved artifact results/processed/native_superflip_upper_bound_probe_seed_2026_compact20.json (root_move_count: 2). If thesis text or numbers are copied from the doc they will contradict the code and saved results.
confirmed.evidence: Doc line ~53: "the current 24-rotation stabilizer reduces first-move representatives from 18 to **3** (`U`, `U'`, `U2`)" and probe rows quoting root_move_count=3, versus test assertion `command[command.index("--root-move-mask") + 1] == "U,U2"` and saved JSON root_move_count=2.
confirmed.repro: grep -n "24-rotation\|root_move_count" docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md; grep -n 'U,U2' tests/test_optimal_native.py; python3 -c "import json; print([r['root_move_count'] for r in json.load(open('results/processed/native_superflip_upper_bound_probe_seed_2026_compact20.json'))['rows']])"
confirmed.suggested_fix: Update section 0.1 to the 48-element group and 2 representatives (or add a dated correction note), and ensure any thesis prose derived from it uses the current numbers.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct reading and a live probe. docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md lines 50-62 (section 0.1) describe a 24-proper-rotation root stabilizer giving 3 superflip first-move representatives (U, U', U2) and quote root_move_count=3 with node counts 1872->354 and 170682->36651. The current code uses the full 48-element group: src/rubik_optimal/symmetry.py:202-203 enforces 48 symmetries, and running root_symmetry_representative_moves on the superflip returns stabilizer size 48 and representatives ('U','U2'). tests/test_optimal_native.py:222-227 asserts mask "U,U2"/root_move_count=2, and results/processed/native_superflip_upper_bound_probe_seed_2026_compact20.json has root_move_count=2 in all rows. A grep tally over results/processed/ finds only root_move_count values 2 and 4 — no saved artifact backs the doc's =3 numbers, so they are unreproducible if copied into the thesis. Mitigations: the doc is a dated chronological log whose later sections (0.5 line 250, 0.9 line 446) correctly state U,U2, section 0.1 explicitly says it uses 24 rotations "not the full 48 ... group", and the thesis chapters contain none of the stale numbers (grep for 354, 36651, root_move in thesis/chapters/*.tex is empty). So this is a stale historical doc entry creating a copy-paste hazard, not a code defect or current thesis contradiction — exactly minor. Fix: add a superseded-by note to section 0.1.

confirmed.title: Node-limit terminations are reported with status "timeout"
confirmed.claim: Both in the upper-bound-proof path and the main IDA* loop, an outcome of kNodeLimit prints status "timeout". This is conservative (never overclaims exactness) but mislabels the stop reason in saved JSON; a node-limited run is indistinguishable from a wall-clock timeout except by comparing expanded_nodes to the requested --node-limit.
confirmed.evidence: Code: `if (outcome == kNodeLimit) { print_solution_json("timeout", ...); return 0; }` in two places, while the solver tracks solver.node_limited separately and never emits it.
confirmed.repro: Run the binary on any scramble with --node-limit 10 and observe status "timeout" in the JSON despite no deadline being hit.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Emit a distinct status (e.g. "node_limit") or include "timed_out"/"node_limited" booleans in the JSON, and surface them in the Python wrapper notes.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by both code re-read and live repro. In native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp, both the upper-bound-proof path (lines 2500-2503) and the main IDA* loop (lines 2550-2553) print status "timeout" when outcome == kNodeLimit, identical to the genuine kTimeout branches. print_solution_json (lines 2301-2378) emits no node_limited or stop-reason field and does not echo --node-limit, even though the solver tracks solver.node_limited (set at lines 1682, 1909, 2125) and never serializes it. Live repro: running native/build/optimal_solver on a 12-move scramble with --timeout 300 --node-limit 10 produced status "timeout" with runtime_seconds 0.103 and expanded_nodes 10 — no deadline was hit. Severity is correctly minor: the conflation is strictly conservative (a "timeout" status never claims exactness, so no optimality overclaim is possible); the thesis explicitly documents the convention that exhausting time or node limit both yield "timeout" (thesis/chapters/03_algorithms.tex:50, 04_implementation.tex:103); and the main evidence script saves the requested node_limit at run level (scripts/run_korf_native_evidence.py:221; results/processed/korf_native_evidence_seed_2026_thesis_native_only.json line 17), so node-limited stops can be inferred post hoc. It remains a real labeling/observability flaw in the native JSON itself (the documented "notes" disambiguation exists only in the Python IDA*, not the native output), but nothing more.

confirmed.title: CLI does not validate cubie ranges or cube invariants; malformed --cp/--co/--ep/--eo input causes undefined behavior instead of a clean error
confirmed.claim: parse_u8_list checks only the element count, not value ranges, and main never checks the twist-sum/flip-sum/parity invariants. A cp value >= 8 or ep value >= 12 leads to out-of-bounds array indexing in rank_permutation8/rank_permutation12 and PDB lookups (undefined behavior, possible crash or garbage heuristic). An invalid-but-in-range state (e.g. single flipped edge) silently searches an unsolvable space until max_depth and reports "lower_bound". No false "exact" is possible (is_solved is unreachable from invalid states and the Python wrapper replays solutions), but the binary is fragile against the malformed input a supervisor might try.
confirmed.evidence: parse_u8_list: `values.push_back(static_cast<std::uint8_t>(std::stoi(item)))` with only `values.size() != expected` checked; rank_permutation8 then computes `1U << value` and indexes kFactorial-based offsets with unvalidated values; no invariant check exists anywhere in main().
confirmed.repro: Reading path: optimal_solver.cpp parse_u8_list (line 2131) and parse_options --cp handling (line 2206); confirm absence of any range or parity validation before corner_coord/edge_subset_coord are evaluated on options.state in main().
confirmed.suggested_fix: After parsing, validate: cp is a permutation of 0..7, ep of 0..11, co in 0..2 with sum % 3 == 0, eo in 0..1 with sum % 2 == 0, and corner parity == edge parity; exit with status "failed" otherwise.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by fresh code read and live execution. (1) parse_u8_list (optimal_solver.cpp:2131-2144) checks only element count; values are cast to uint8_t unvalidated, and parse_options (2206-2217) copies them into options.state with no range check. (2) main() (2382-2583) contains no twist-sum/flip-sum/parity invariant check before the state reaches corner_coord/edge_subset_coord/inverse_state. (3) Live probe: --ep "0,...,10,250" with an edge PDB loaded segfaults (exit 139, no JSON) — e2s[state.ep[position]] at line 1224 indexes the 12-entry edge_to_subset table out of bounds; this is a reproducible crash, stronger than the reviewer's "possible crash". --cp with value 200 emits garbage (initial_lower_bound 10, status lower_bound) via the UB shift 1U<<value in rank_permutation8 (line 910). A single-flipped-edge state (valid ranges, broken invariant) silently searches to max_depth and exits 0 with status "lower_bound", exactly as claimed. One mechanism nitpick: rank_permutation8/12 themselves do only arithmetic (the OOB indexing happens at the downstream PDB lookup and in edge_subset_coord/inverse_state — line 1268 is even an OOB stack write for cp>=8), but the substance is correct. Mitigations also verified: is_solved is unreachable from invalid states (moves only permute cubie values), and the Python wrapper (optimal_native.py:291) replays "exact" solutions via verify_solution, so no false exactness and no thesis number is affected — all experiments pass validated CubeState objects. Severity "minor" is right: real fragility against direct malformed CLI input, but zero impact on reported results or optimality claims.

confirmed.title: Default auto-inclusion of 7-edge PDBs silently changes the korf_native_optimal heuristic configuration relative to previously saved benchmark results
confirmed.claim: use_seven_edge_pdbs defaults to True, so any rerun of an experiment script that calls solve_korf_native_optimal now loads 8 six-edge PDBs plus the two 510 MB 7-edge PDBs whenever they exist in data/generated. Solution lengths are unaffected (all components admissible), but expanded/generated node counts, runtimes, and table_bytes are no longer comparable to earlier saved results (e.g. korf_native_evidence_seed_2026_thesis_native_only.json) produced before the 7-edge tables existed. A thesis table mixing old and regenerated rows would show inconsistent node counts for the "same" solver, and prose describing the Korf heuristic as corner + 6-edge PDBs would no longer match the artifact's default behavior.
confirmed.evidence: optimal_native.py line 146: `use_seven_edge_pdbs: bool = True`; lines 167-169 silently append default_edge_pdb_paths_7 whenever edge_pdbs_7_available(); both thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_pdb.bin and thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_5_6_7_8_9_10_11_pdb.bin (510,935,112 bytes each) are present in data/generated, so the new default is active on this machine.
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=src python3 -c "from rubik_optimal.cube import CubeState; from rubik_optimal.solvers.optimal_native import solve_korf_native_optimal; r=solve_korf_native_optimal(CubeState.from_sequence('R U'), threads=1); print([n for n in r.notes.split('; ') if n.startswith('edge_pdb_count')])"  (prints edge_pdb_count=10, not 8)
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pin use_seven_edge_pdbs explicitly in every experiment script that feeds thesis tables, and record edge_pdb_count/table_bytes in each saved row (already in notes) so the thesis states which configuration each benchmark used.

votes.reasoning: CONFIRMED by direct code re-read and execution. (1) /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/solvers/optimal_native.py:146 has `use_seven_edge_pdbs: bool = True`, and lines 167-169 silently append default_edge_pdb_paths_7 whenever both 7-edge files exist; lines 269-272 pass all of them as `--edge-pdb`. Both thesis_seed_2026_edge_subset_0_1_2_3_4_5_6_pdb.bin and ..._5_6_7_8_9_10_11_pdb.bin (510,935,112 bytes each, created Jun 9 00:02/00:30) exist in data/generated, so the default is active. (2) I ran the repro: solve_korf_native_optimal on 'R U' returns edge_pdb_count=10 and table_bytes=1,450,674,056. (3) The reviewer's example artifact is slightly misattributed - korf_native_evidence_seed_2026_thesis_native_only.json (Jun 8, edge_pdb_bytes=340,623,936 = 8 six-edge tables) is generated by the PYTHON solve_korf_ida via scripts/run_korf_native_evidence.py, not solve_korf_native_optimal. But this does not refute the finding; it widens it: heuristics.py:116 unconditionally folds `edge_pdb7` into heuristic_lower_bound_components/combined_table_lower_bound, so the Python Korf path also silently changed, and src/rubik_optimal/solvers/korf.py:62-63 computes table_bytes WITHOUT the 7-edge bytes while the heuristic uses them - a regenerated artifact would be internally inconsistent (heuristic label 'max(misplaced cubie, coordinate pruning, corner PDB, edge PDB)' at run_korf_native_evidence.py:139 also omits the 7-edge component). (4) The direct solve_korf_native_optimal artifact is results/processed/optimal_3x3_seed_2026_thesis.json (Jun 8, every row edge_pdb_count=8, table_size_bytes=428,803,832), which the thesis cites at thesis/chapters/05_experiments.tex:23 and whose table thesis/tables/optimal_3x3_status.tex is \input at line 124, immediately after prose stating the script calls the solver 'me ti goniaki PDB kai tis okto edge PDBs' (with the corner PDB and the EIGHT edge PDBs) and printing the exact rerun command `python scripts/run_3x3_optimal.py --profile thesis --seed 2026`. Rerunning that exact printed command today uses 10 edge PDBs, contradicting the prose, and the table's 'Initial LB' and 'Seconds' columns could change (the 7-edge tables can raise the MAX bound on deeper rows). The abstract (00_abstracts.tex:23) also describes only 'native six-edge pattern databases'; no thesis chapter mentions the 7-edge tables (grep for seven/7-edge in thesis/chapters returns nothing). Saved artifacts already show silent config drift across this default mechanism (optimal_3x3_seed_2026_stress.json from May 26 has edge_pdb_count=4). Optimality/solution lengths are unaffected (all components admissible via MAX), so this is not critical, but I upgrade minor->major: the thesis's documented reproduction command no longer reproduces the published configuration, a printed numeric column (Initial LB) can change on regeneration, thesis prose contradicts the artifact's current default behavior, and the Python evidence path would regenerate with an internally inconsistent table_bytes/heuristic description. Fix is cheap: default use_seven_edge_pdbs=False (opt-in), or record the PDB configuration in artifacts and update prose.

confirmed.title: Certificate store silently keeps the shorter of two conflicting 'exact' lengths for the same state
confirmed.claim: _add_best replaces an existing certificate when a new one for the same facelet state has a strictly smaller solution_length. Two saved rows both flagged status='exact' but with different lengths for the same state is a soundness contradiction (at least one exactness flag was wrong); the code resolves it silently by preferring the shorter, hiding the inconsistency from audit instead of surfacing it.
confirmed.evidence: exact_certificates.py:220-223: 'previous = certificates.get(certificate.state); if previous is None or certificate.solution_length < previous.solution_length: certificates[certificate.state] = certificate' — no warning, log, or error on a length conflict between two exact rows.
confirmed.repro: Read _add_best and note it is called from _load/_add_symmetry_closure/remember_result for every row; construct two artifact rows for the same state with status='exact' and lengths L and L-1 and observe both load without complaint.
confirmed.suggested_fix: When previous is not None and lengths differ, record/raise a certificate-conflict diagnostic (and ideally invalidate both until re-proved) instead of silently merging.

votes.reasoning: Code re-read confirms the finding verbatim: src/rubik_optimal/exact_certificates.py:219-223 (_add_best) resolves a solution_length conflict between two status='exact' rows for the same facelet state by silently keeping the shorter, with no warning/log/error; it is called from _load (198, 202), _add_symmetry_closure (265), and remember_result (174, 178), and no other guard catches the contradiction (length validity is re-checked in _certificate_from_row, but that only bounds distance from above). Empirical repro (script at /tmp/probe_cert_conflict_v2.py): two exact/verified rows for the state reached by 'R U' with valid solutions of lengths 4 and 2 both load without complaint and the shorter silently wins in either insertion order. Since exact distance is unique, such a conflict proves at least one upstream exactness flag is wrong, and the store hides this free soundness tripwire. Mitigating facts that keep it minor: (1) scanning all 30 present default artifacts found 218 exact rows / 160 states with zero direct-row length conflicts, and an instrumented full _load including inverse+symmetry closure (7562 certificates) also hit zero conflicts, so no current thesis number is affected; (2) the module docstring (lines 3-18) explicitly documents that optimality is inherited, not re-derived; (3) preferring the shorter row is the only defensible resolution direction (the shorter valid solution definitively refutes the longer row's exactness), so the returned value is never made worse by the rule. The defect is the silence, not the choice. Recommended fix: warn or raise when previous.solution_length != certificate.solution_length in _add_best.

confirmed.title: AttributeError on the failure path of benchmark-certificate verification (VerificationResult has no .reason)
confirmed.claim: On a failed replay the code does 'raise ValueError(verification.reason or ...)', but VerificationResult (src/rubik_optimal/verify.py:11-15) defines fields ok/move_count/message — there is no .reason attribute. The resulting AttributeError is swallowed by the broad 'except Exception' at line 85, so the row is correctly rejected, but the recorded error string is a misleading AttributeError instead of the verification message.
confirmed.evidence: verify.py:12-15 defines 'ok: bool; move_count: int; message: str'; import script line 84 references 'verification.reason'.
confirmed.repro: python3 -c "from rubik_optimal.verify import VerificationResult; VerificationResult(False,0,'x').reason" → AttributeError. Trace the except at scripts/import_nissy_benchmark_certificates.py:85.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change verification.reason to verification.message.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct re-read and live repro. src/rubik_optimal/verify.py:11-15 defines VerificationResult with fields ok/move_count/message only; accessing .reason raises AttributeError (verified: python3 -c "from rubik_optimal.verify import VerificationResult; VerificationResult(False,0,'x').reason"). scripts/import_nissy_benchmark_certificates.py:84 references verification.reason on the not-ok branch; this is the sole VerificationResult definition and sole .reason usage in the repo, so no alternate convention or stale version explains it. Traced the path with a simulated failing verification: the AttributeError is caught by 'except Exception as exc' at line 85 and the recorded error string becomes "distance D offset I: 'VerificationResult' object has no attribute 'reason'" instead of the verification message. Impact is limited exactly as claimed: the row is still rejected, errors are recorded, passed=False, exit code 1 — the importer fails closed, and the branch is nearly unreachable anyway (the solution is the literal inverse of the scramble, so verify_solution can only fail if the move engine is broken). Only the diagnostic string is wrong, so 'minor' is the correct severity. Fix: use verification.message at line 84.

confirmed.title: Chapter 05 verbatim reproduction command does not match saved artifact parameters (--hard-timeout 20 vs 30)

confirmed.title: measure_heuristic_strength.py produces no saved artifact and its checks are weak, while its conclusions drive design decisions

(a) 06_discussion.tex:11 says timeouts remain on the Korf/IDA* track for the depth-15 and depth-20 random cases, but benchmarks_seed_2026_thesis.csv records korf_ida_star_scoped status=lower_bound for random_2_15 and random_3_20 (notes 'No exact solution within configured depth', only 72/154 expanded nodes — depth-exhausted, not time/node-limited), and the thesis's own tables agree with the data: solver_status_counts.tex:5 shows Korf with 2 lower_bound / 0 timeout, benchmark_timeout_cases.tex:6 says 'No timeout rows'. The prose contradicts the thesis's own definition of timeout (06_discussion.tex:13: stop due to time or node limit). (b) 03_algorithms.tex:50 claims the result is 'timeout' when the initial lower bound exceeds the allowed depth, but src/rubik_optimal/search/ida_star.py:105-109 returns status 'lower_bound' for that case (and 188-191 for depth exhaustion); 'timeout' is only returned for wall-clock/node-budget exhaustion (174-183). The korf.py docstring (51-53) matches the code, not the chapter. Alternative reading (native solver) refuted: the paragraph describes the Python IDA* track, and optimal_native.py:157 distinguishes the same statuses. (c) 05_experiments.tex:458 legend lists only E/N/T/NA/F; benchmark_case_status_matrix.tex:16-17 uses 'LB' (mapped from lower_bound at src/rubik_optimal/benchmark.py:294-301), LB is defined nowhere in the chapter, and no T appears in the matrix. Severity: minor is right — the defect is purely textual; saved data, generated tables, and code are internally consistent and honest, and mislabeling lower_bound as timeout understates rather than overstates results. confirmed.title: Unit slip: raw phase-1 table called '2,2 GiB' (actual 2.07 GiB / 2.22 GB)
confirmed.claim: The text says the compressed table is '~141 MB έναντι 2,2 GiB του ωμού πίνακα'. The raw table is 2048*2187*495 = 2,217,093,120 entries (file is 2,217,093,144 bytes) = 2.065 GiB = 2.217 GB. '2.2' is correct only in GB, not GiB; meanwhile '141 MB' uses decimal MB (140,908,434 bytes = 134.4 MiB). Mixing the bases inflates the claimed compression baseline slightly.
confirmed.evidence: ls -la data/generated/kociemba_phase1_full_depth9.bin -> 2,217,093,144 bytes (= 2.065 GiB); data/generated/kociemba_phase1_sym_depth12.bin -> 140,908,434 bytes.

confirmed.suggested_fix: Write '~141 MB έναντι ~2,2 GB' (or '~134 MiB έναντι ~2,1 GiB') consistently.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct reading and measurement. thesis/chapters/04_implementation.tex:117 says "περίπου 141 MB έναντι 2,2 GiB του ωμού πίνακα". The raw phase-1 table is 2048*2187*495 = 2,217,093,120 entries; the file data/generated/kociemba_phase1_full_depth9.bin is 2,217,093,144 bytes = 2.065 GiB = 2.217 GB. So "2,2" is correct only in decimal GB, not GiB (2.2 GiB would be ~2.36 GB, ~7% larger than reality; the correct GiB figure rounds to 2.1). The "141 MB" on the other side of the same comparison is decimal (140,908,434 bytes = 134.4 MiB), and the same sentence uses "16 GiB" correctly for RAM, so the unit mixing is internally inconsistent. The same "2.2 GiB" phrasing is echoed in scripts/probe_native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip.py:230, docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md:392 and :428, and docs/limitations.md:49. The accompanying "~16x" compression claim is fine in consistent units (true ratio 15.73x), so the defect is purely the unit label. Fix: write "2,2 GB" or "2,1 GiB" (and align the doc/script strings). Severity minor is appropriate: presentation-level units slip, no impact on correctness or benchmark numbers.

confirmed.title: Resolved: the 'fix native-optimal test constant' was a legitimate test fix, not expectation-tuning — but the commit message misdescribes the commit
confirmed.claim: The assignment asked whether commit de5b9d1's test-constant change adjusted a failing expectation to match the code. It did not: the old expectation (optimal length 4 for F R U R' U') was wrong in the TEST, the solver's answer of 5 was correct, and the fix replaced the hand-guessed constant with an exhaustive BFS oracle — the right direction. Two hygiene problems remain: (a) de5b9d1's message claims it changed tests/test_optimal_native.py and .gitignore, but its diff contains only docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md (the test fix is actually inside the squashed baseline a57fa81), so the history misdescribes itself; (b) 355 lines of new wrapper-forwarding tests in test_optimal_native.py and 55 lines in test_symmetry.py are uncommitted working-tree modifications.
confirmed.evidence: Independent verification: meet-in-the-middle probe with the repo cube model — radius-2 ball around CubeState.from_sequence("F R U R' U'") (262 states) and radius-2 ball around solved (262 states) are disjoint => distance > 4; radius-3 solved ball intersects => distance <= 5; hence exactly 5. The cube model itself is externally anchored: the standard superflip generator 'U R2 F B R B2 R U2 L B2 R U' D' R2 F R' L B2 U2 F2' reproduces the all-edges-flipped cubie state. 'git show de5b9d1 --stat' lists only docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md; 'git show a57fa81:tests/test_optimal_native.py' already contains the BFS-oracle version; 'git diff HEAD --stat -- tests/' shows +355/+55 uncommitted lines.
confirmed.repro: git show de5b9d1 --stat; git show a57fa81:tests/test_optimal_native.py | sed -n '160,200p'; PYTHONPATH=src python with the meet-in-the-middle snippet above.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Commit the pending test additions and, if history is ever rewritten before submission, correct the de5b9d1 message (or note in docs that the test fix landed in the squashed baseline).

votes.reasoning: Every element of the finding survives independent re-verification. (1) Resolution of the original expectation-tuning question is correct: an independent meet-in-the-middle probe with the repo cube model (anchored by the superflip generator reproducing the all-edges-flipped cubie state) proves F R U R' U' is at exact HTM distance 5 — radius-2 balls around target and solved (262 states each) are disjoint (>4), and the radius-2 target ball intersects the radius-3 solved ball (<=5). So the solver's 5 was right, the old test constant 4 was the bug, and the current test (tests/test_optimal_native.py:446-478) correctly uses an exhaustive BFS oracle with an explicit status=="exact" guard instead of a hand-guessed constant. (2) Commit-message misdescription confirmed: `git show de5b9d1 --stat` lists only docs/WORSTCASE_HEURISTIC_DESIGN.md (+152), yet the message claims changes to tests/test_optimal_native.py and .gitignore; both of those changes are already verbatim in the squashed baseline a57fa81 (BFS-oracle test at line 168 of a57fa81:tests/test_optimal_native.py; .gitignore lines 41-44 ignoring the .js workflow scripts), and `git diff a57fa81 de5b9d1 -- .gitignore tests/` is empty. (3) Uncommitted test additions confirmed: `git diff HEAD --stat -- tests/` shows +355 lines in test_optimal_native.py (wrapper-forwarding/monkeypatched fake_run tests plus two BFS-matching native tests) and +55 in test_symmetry.py. Caveat: the whole working tree is mid-flight (23 modified files, many untracked), so (3) is ordinary in-progress state, not a distinct defect. Nothing affects thesis numbers or optimality claims, so 'minor' is the right severity; remediation is a trivial commit-message reword/note plus committing the new tests when ready.

confirmed.title: No test anchors the cube move model to an external convention
confirmed.claim: Every distance/optimality test uses BFS over the project's own move model as the oracle, and the superflip is always constructed directly as a cubie state (eo=(1,)*12) — no test ever applies a published generator sequence or pins a known facelet string for a single move. An internally consistent but conventionally wrong move model (e.g. mirrored F/B) would pass the entire suite while making thesis statements like 'this facelet string is at distance d' wrong relative to the standard Singmaster convention. I verified by probe that the model is in fact correct, so this is a missing-test gap, not a latent bug.
confirmed.evidence: grep for the standard superflip generator 'U R2 F B R B2...' across tests/, src/, scripts/ returns nothing; tests/test_h48_native.py:70-76 builds superflip directly from cubie fields. Probe: CubeState.from_sequence("U R2 F B R B2 R U2 L B2 R U' D' R2 F R' L B2 U2 F2") == CubeState(cp=identity, co=0, ep=identity, eo=(1,)*12) returned True.
confirmed.repro: grep -rn "R2 F B R B2" /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/tests /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/scripts (no hits); then run the probe one-liner above with PYTHONPATH=src.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a two-line test: the published superflip generator applied to solved equals the directly-constructed superflip cubie state (and optionally pin to_facelets() of a single R move to a hardcoded literal). This externally anchors the move semantics that every other test implicitly trusts.

votes.reasoning: The core gap is real and I confirmed it by re-reading the cited code and re-running probes. (1) grep confirms no published superflip generator anywhere in tests/src/scripts; tests always construct superflip directly from cubie fields (tests/test_h48_native.py:70-76, tests/test_symmetry.py:63, tests/test_optimal_native.py:213). (2) tests/test_moves_and_cube.py:8-96 contains only self-referential properties (round-trips, inverses, move orders); no facelet string is pinned for any single move. (3) I verified the model is in fact correct: the published generator "U R2 F B R B2 R U2 L B2 R U' D' R2 F R' L B2 U2 F2" reproduces eo=(1,)*12, and from_sequence("F"/"U"/"R").to_facelets() match the standard URFDLB Singmaster strings — so this is a missing-test gap, not a latent bug. One correction to the finding's framing: the claim that EVERY distance test uses the project's own BFS as oracle is overbroad. Tests at tests/test_h48_native.py:136-153, 442-462, 640-684, 703-730 run the real vendored third-party nissy-core binary and verify its standard-notation solutions with the project's own move model (verify_solution at src/rubik_optimal/solvers/h48_native.py:208,225; I ran test_h48_native_solves_non_solved_cube_without_source_sequence — passes in 0.36s against the real binary), which externally anchors the cubie-to-nissy mapping (pinned at test_h48_native.py:66-67) and the R/U quarter-turn directions. But these tests only ever use scrambles "R", "R U", "R U F2", whose verified solutions contain only R/U quarter turns and F2, so the reviewer's specific hypothetical (mirrored F/B — and likewise L/D) would indeed pass the entire suite, since half-turns are direction-invariant and the certificate-store tests use synthetic tmp-path artifacts rather than the real saved deep-solution files. Net: the finding stands, with the scope narrowed to "F/B/L/D quarter-turn chirality and the full 18-move set are unanchored by any test; R/U/F2 are anchored via real nissy-core round-trips." Fix is trivial (one test asserting the published superflip generator equals the cubie superflip, or pinning a single-move facelet string). Severity "minor" is correct: it cannot change any reported thesis number today, but it is exactly the convention question a supervisor would probe.

confirmed.title: 'infrastructure' marker is registered twice but applied to zero tests — documented deselection is a no-op
confirmed.claim: conftest.py and pyproject.toml ([tool.pytest.ini_options] markers, line 36) both register an 'infrastructure' marker described as gating cloud/oracle scaffolding tests, but no test in the repo carries @pytest.mark.infrastructure or a pytestmark. confirmed.title: Golden values asserted without independent derivation (edge-projection ball layers, superflip phase-1 distance)
confirmed.claim: Several expected constants appear to be captured from the implementation's own first run rather than derived independently: the canonical-rotation ball layer counts [1, 13, 142, 1798] and full-symmetry counts [1, 9, 80, 932] in test_edge_projection_probe.py, and superflip_phase1_sym_dist == 10 in test_kociemba_phase1_symmetry.py:78 (comment 'fixed by the reduction' offers no derivation). These work as regression pins but cannot detect an initially-wrong implementation. Mitigation exists for the phase-1 table (the 64,430 class count and diameter 12 match Kociemba's published values, and a separate test cross-checks >900M entries against the raw BFS table up to depth 9), but the depth-10 superflip value and the probe layer counts have no external or cross-implementation anchor.
confirmed.evidence: tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py:120 'assert [layer["seen"] for layer in payload["layers"]] == [1, 13, 142, 1798]' with no derivation comment; tests/test_kociemba_phase1_symmetry.py:77-78 '# The superflip's phase-1 distance is exactly 10 (fixed by the reduction).' followed by the bare constant.
confirmed.repro: Read tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py:96-150 and tests/test_kociemba_phase1_symmetry.py:66-79; note no independent computation backs the constants (unlike e.g. 64430/12, which match published Kociemba values, or 510,935,040 = 12!/5!*2^7).
confirmed.suggested_fix: For the ball layers, add a slow-path cross-check at depth <= 2 computed in pure Python (expand all 18^d move sequences, project, canonicalize, count) so at least the first layers are independently derived; for the superflip phase-1 distance, cross-check via the existing Python phase-1 IDA* (solve_kociemba_phase1 at max_depth 10 succeeds, at 9 with full exhaustion fails).

votes.reasoning: Code re-read confirms every citation. tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py:120,150 assert bare constants [1,13,142,1798] / [1,9,80,932] against the native probe's own output with no derivation comment, and no test pins the raw (no-symmetry) ball whose layers (18,243,3240) would match published full-cube counts. tests/test_kociemba_phase1_symmetry.py:78 asserts superflip_phase1_sym_dist == 10 with only "(fixed by the reduction)" as justification; the raw cross-check table (kociemba_phase1_full_depth9.bin) covers distances <= 9 only, so the depth-10 superflip entry is exactly outside the byte-for-byte verified region, as claimed. Grep shows the value 10 has no other source in the repo besides the native emitter computed from the same sym tables under test — no cross-implementation anchor. I then tried to break the finding the other way by independently deriving all three constants from first principles (scripts in /tmp, not the repo): (1) the canonical/fullsym ball counts must equal orbit counts of the raw depth-3 ball under the stabilizer of the excluded edge pair {BL,BR} (order 2 within 24 rotations, order 4 within 48 symmetries, since HTM moves are closed under conjugation and the projection is injective on the radius-3 ball); my from-scratch implementation reproduces [1,13,142,1798] and [1,9,80,932] exactly. (2) An independent flipUDslice BFS (1,013,760 states) plus IDA* with admissible max(flipUDslice, CO) heuristic gives the superflip phase-1 distance exactly 10 (bounds 8 and 9 exhausted, witness at 10). So the pinned values are all CORRECT — the finding is real as a test-methodology gap (golden values that could not have detected an initially wrong implementation), but has no impact on reported results, and the ball constants do not appear in the thesis chapters. "Minor" is the right severity; the reviewer also correctly credited the existing mitigations (64,430 classes, diameter 12, >900M-entry raw cross-check). A trivial nit: the asserted numbers are cumulative "seen" counts (per-layer frontiers are 12/129/1656 and 8/71/852), which does not change the finding. Suggested fix for the student: add the stabilizer-orbit derivation (or a small Python cross-check) for the ball counts and an independent phase-1 computation (or literature/derivation note) for the superflip value of 10 — my probes show both are cheap (<1 min).

confirmed.title: No per-test timeout protection in the suite (pytest-timeout not installed)

Not currently biting: no hangs were observed in this run; the slowest single test was tests/test_kociemba_phase1_symmetry.py::test_sym_phase1_matches_raw_bfs_on_all_known_entries at 123.87s, and several native tests pass their own timeout_seconds to the solvers.
