confirmed.title: solve_kociemba_native_scoped labels a timed-out run 'lower_bound' when phase 2 was never attempted
confirmed.claim: The no-solution return uses status = best_phase2_for_diag.status if best_phase2_for_diag is not None else "lower_bound". When the deadline expires before any phase-2 attempt (best_phase2_for_diag is None, phase1_candidates_tried=0), the run actually timed out but is reported as 'lower_bound'. The repo elsewhere (search/ida_star.py lines 176-190, distance.py) deliberately distinguishes timeout from lower_bound, and AGENTS.md forbids presenting timed-out output as a bound-establishing outcome.
confirmed.evidence: Observed live: scramble "F' B U L2 R2 B' R F2" with defaults returned status='lower_bound' after running the full 10s timeout with notes 'phase1_candidates_collected=7; phase1_candidates_tried=0; best_phase2_lower_bound=None' - no phase-2 search ever ran, so no depth-bounded exhaustion supports a 'lower_bound' label.
confirmed.repro: Same one-liner as finding 1 (defaults branch); inspect r.status and r.notes.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Return status='timeout' when the deadline expired with phase1_candidates_tried == 0 (or whenever time, not depth exhaustion, ended the search).

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by code re-read and live reproduction. In solve_kociemba_native_scoped (src/rubik_optimal/solvers/kociemba.py), collect_kociemba_phase1_candidates receives the full timeout budget (line 724) and can consume all of it while still returning candidates with status "exact" (lines 546-556, note "timeout reached after collecting candidates"). The candidate loop then immediately breaks on remaining<=0 (lines 799-801) before any phase-2 attempt, leaving best_phase2_for_diag=None, and line 869 labels the result "lower_bound" via the else branch. Reproduced deterministically: scramble "F' B U L2 R2 B' R F2" with timeout_seconds=1.0 returns status='lower_bound' with notes 'phase1_candidates_collected=7; phase1_candidates_tried=0; best_phase2_lower_bound=None' — exactly matching the reviewer's observation (their 10s-default repro is timing-dependent; on this machine defaults solved in 3.5s, but the path is identical). The repo's convention is confirmed: "lower_bound" is reserved for depth-bound exhaustion and "timeout" for time/node exhaustion (ida_star.py lines 176-190; kociemba.py's own phase functions; distance.py lines 73/107 map timeout to "unknown_timeout"), and oracle.py/probe scripts treat status=="lower_bound" as a proof condition, so the label is load-bearing. Severity "minor" is correct: it is a real labeling defect on the no-solution fallback of a solver that claims no optimality, and all 13 kociemba_native_scoped rows in results/processed/benchmarks_seed_2026_thesis.csv have status 'non_exact', so no saved thesis number is currently affected.

confirmed.title: recognize_distance h48 branch maps solver_status='failed' to kind='lower_bound'
confirmed.claim: In the h48 branch, any non-exact non-timeout solver status -- including 'failed' (backend crash, bad table, preload failure) -- yields kind='lower_bound'. The numeric value is still sound because it comes from the in-process admissible combined_table_lower_bound, not from the failed solver, but the kind conflates 'backend failed' with 'completed bounded search excluded shorter solutions', which downstream consumers filtering on kind cannot distinguish.
confirmed.evidence: Observed live: recognize_distance(superflip, h48_native=True, h48_solver='h48h7', h48_preload_table=True, timeout_seconds=150) returned kind=lower_bound, dist=8, with notes 'solver_status=failed' after 127.2s. Code: '"unknown_timeout" if optimal.status == "timeout" else "lower_bound"' (distance.py:107).
confirmed.repro: PYTHONPATH=/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src /opt/miniconda3/bin/python3 /tmp/xval_superflip7.py with h48_preload_table=True, or force a failed status by pointing h48_table_path at a bogus file.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a distinct kind (e.g. 'unknown_failed') or reuse 'unknown_timeout'-style wording for failed backends; keep 'lower_bound' for completed depth-bounded searches only. Note the same pattern exists in the native_optimal branch (distance.py:73).

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code re-read and a live forced-failure probe. distance.py:107 reads exactly as quoted: '"unknown_timeout" if optimal.status == "timeout" else "lower_bound"'. The h48 solver emits statuses including "failed" (h48_native.py:224, 402, 796, 1196) and "not_applicable" (h48_native.py:697, 888), all of which fall into the else branch and yield kind="lower_bound". Reproduced in ~5s: recognize_distance with h48_native=True and h48_table_path=/tmp/nonexistent_h48_table.bin returns kind=lower_bound, notes "solver_status=failed; missing required H48 table". The "kind describes the value" defense fails because timeout — whose value is equally an admissible lower bound — gets its own kind "unknown_timeout", showing kind is meant to encode the failure mode; "failed" should be distinguished likewise. Impact is limited, matching the claimed minor severity: the numeric value is sound (computed in-process by combined_table_lower_bound at line 104, independent of the failed solver), proof_notes carries solver_status=failed, no saved result file in results/processed/ contains solver_status=failed (so no thesis number affected), and in-repo consumers (run_distance_recognition_corpus.py kind-matching; benchmark.py kind/method LaTeX counts) would at worst mislabel a crashed run as a bounded lower bound, never upgrade it to exact. The same pattern exists in the native_optimal branch at distance.py:73, which the student may want to fix in the same pass.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code re-read and inspection of the current payload. (1) Code: /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/scripts/thesis_audit.py main() (lines 4195-4311) computes checks, acceptance_scale_passed, acceptance_implementation_passed, final_submission_ready, and submission_blockers (lines 4244-4304), writes them into the JSON payload, and then the ONLY exit-code logic is lines 4309-4311: `if not pdf_deliverable["passed"]: return 1 / return 0`. No other field influences the return value; `__main__` is `raise SystemExit(main())` (line 4315), so the int is the process exit code with no other guard. (2) Current state: results/processed/thesis_audit.json (the saved output of the latest run) has pdf_deliverable.passed=true, acceptance_scale_passed=false, acceptance_implementation_passed=false, final_submission_ready=false, 5 submission_blockers, and failed checks git_provenance_clean and source_state_reproducible — so the deterministic execution path returns 0 exactly as the reviewer observed. I did not re-run the script myself only because it rewrites results/processed/thesis_audit.json inside the repo (prohibited), but the path from payload construction to return is straight-line code with no exceptions possible from the failing booleans. (3) The exit code is actually consumed in this repo: archived pipeline scripts (e.g. archive/results/cloud_hardtail_runbook_cloud_20260531_p10/finalize_full_after_collect.sh, lines 2 and 12/15) run `set -euo pipefail` then `python scripts/thesis_audit.py`, so a failing audit would not abort those pipelines — they proceed as if the audit passed. Possible refutation considered: this could be an intentional contract where consumers parse the JSON rather than the exit code, and only PDF staleness is treated as a hard error; but nothing in the script or docs documents such a contract, and a script named thesis_audit.py exiting 0 while reporting final_submission_ready=false is a real footgun for any scripted gate. Severity: "minor" is correct — it changes no reported thesis numbers and the JSON payload truthfully records the failures; it is a process/tooling defect, not a soundness defect.

confirmed.title: recognize_distance reports a needlessly weak lower bound after a completed depth-bounded IDA* (7 reported where >= 11 was proven)
confirmed.claim: When ida_star_solve completes all bounds up to ida_depth (10 here) without timeout and finds no solution, the search has proven distance >= ida_depth + 1 = 11, but the code returns distance_value = combined_table_lower_bound(cube) (7 in the saved artifact). The result is still a valid lower bound, so no soundness violation, but the artifact prints 'lower_bound 7' for a state known to be at distance ~20 while discarding the strictly stronger bound the run itself established. The notes text acknowledges the depth-bounded search completed, so the information is there but not folded into the value.
confirmed.evidence: distance.py line 128: 'distance_value = combined_table_lower_bound(cube)' followed by the non-timeout branch at lines 143-155 returning that value with kind 'lower_bound' and a note saying the depth-bounded search completed; saved JSON deep rows: distance_kind='lower_bound', distance_value=7, distance_method='combined_table_lower_bound' for a 20-move scramble searched exhaustively to depth 10.
confirmed.repro: Read src/rubik_optimal/distance.py lines 113-155: the completed-IDA* branch never computes max(table_bound, ida_depth + 1). Cross-check the saved JSON deep rows (distance_value 7, kind lower_bound).
confirmed.suggested_fix: In the non-timeout branch return max(combined_table_lower_bound(cube), ida_depth + 1) (the +1 is justified only when the IDA* loop genuinely exhausted bound == ida_depth without truncation; ida_star_solve already distinguishes that case via status 'lower_bound') and record both components in the notes.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by code re-read and live reproduction. distance.py:128,143-155 returns combined_table_lower_bound(cube) with kind 'lower_bound' when ida_star_solve completes without timeout. ida_star.py guarantees the 'lower_bound' status is only returned after the final bound iteration finished with neither timeout nor node-budget exhaustion (checks at lines 174-183 precede the break), the goal test precedes the depth cutoff (lines 130-133), and the same-face/commuting prunings preserve a canonical optimal path — so a clean completion proves distance >= ida_depth + 1, which the code discards instead of taking max(table_bound, ida_depth + 1). The saved artifact results/processed/e2e_3x3_seed_2026_thesis.json shows the deep rows (ida_depth=10 per scripts/run_3x3_end_to_end.py:59) with kind 'lower_bound' and value 7, i.e. >= 11 was proven but 7 was saved. Live rerun of the identical scramble reproduces the mechanism: IDA* completes in 1.9 s / 94 expanded nodes with status 'lower_bound', yet recognize_distance returns the table bound (now 9 under the working-tree heuristic) instead of 11. No soundness violation: the value is a valid admissible lower bound, the notes are honest, and the thesis table prints only the kind, not the number — so 'minor' is the right severity. Fix is one line: distance_value = max(distance_value, ida_depth + 1) in the completed branch (correct even in the h(start) > ida_depth early-return case).

confirmed.title: `solve` exits 0 even when the result status is timeout or failed

confirmed.title: `oracle --universal` silently forces trusted_table=True, contradicting the appendix's opt-in framing of --h48-trusted-table
confirmed.line: 1290 (stream universal: trusted_table=True hardcoded), 1476 (batch: effective_trusted_h48_table = bool(args.h48_trusted_table or args.universal))

The thesis contradicts this: d_cli_reference.tex line 4 presents --h48-trusted-table as the option that skips the full table check, line 31 frames it as opt-in after checksum verification while recommending the very demo paths (distance --h48-oracle, oracle) where trusted mode is forced without the flag, and 04_implementation.tex line 98 calls the flag 'προαιρετικό' (optional). Mitigations confirmed: every skip_table_check path calls validate_trusted_h48_table (tables/h48.py:279; solvers/h48_native.py:306/913/1477/...) and fails closed on bad metadata, and batch oracle output records the effective value (cli.py:1810), so there is no soundness risk and no impact on reported numbers. Tests at test_cli_and_results.py ~199/455/597 assert trusted_table is True, encoding the hidden default. Real but low-impact documentation/behavior divergence; 'minor' severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: --max-depth is silently clamped/repurposed differently per solver
confirmed.claim: For optimal-native/h48-native/h48-oracle/race/universal/nissy-core-direct the CLI passes max_depth=max(args.max_depth, 20) (a user-supplied lower value is silently ignored); for auto/native-kociemba it becomes phase1=max(v,10) and phase2=max(v+4,14); for korf it is used raw (default 10). The flag's help text ('--max-depth', no description) does not explain any of this, so e.g. `solve --solver optimal-native --max-depth 5` behaves identically to --max-depth 20 with no warning, while `--solver korf --max-depth 5` genuinely limits depth.

confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a help string documenting the per-solver clamping, or reject values below the solver's minimum instead of silently raising them.

votes.reasoning: Direct re-read of src/rubik_optimal/cli.py confirms every cited line: line 224 defines --max-depth (default 10) with no help text; lines 907, 939, 951, 974, 998, 1139 pass max_depth=max(args.max_depth, 20) for optimal-native/h48-native/h48-oracle/race-optimal/resident-race-optimal/universal-optimal/nissy-core-direct, silently ignoring any user value below 20; lines 900-901 and 924-925 repurpose it as phase1=max(v,10)/phase2=max(v+4,14) for auto and native-kociemba; line 1171 (korf) and 1176 (bfs) use it raw. Thistlethwaite (lines 1161-1162) adds yet another semantics (floors 13/15), and the max(...,20) pattern recurs at lines 1289, 1433, 1491, 1636, 1677. Grep finds no warning/clamp message anywhere in cli.py, so the override is silent. The clamp is unconditional in the argument expression, so `--max-depth 5` is provably identical to `--max-depth 20` for the optimal solvers; no guard clause was missed. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: the >=20 clamp actually protects optimality soundness (God's number is 20), so no thesis result is affected; the defect is undocumented, inconsistent, silent flag semantics — a CLI quality issue, not a correctness one.

confirmed.title: source_state fails open: a failing `git status` is stamped as a clean, reproducible checkout
confirmed.claim: `status_lines = status_proc.stdout.splitlines() if status_proc and status_proc.returncode == 0 else []` maps a failed/None `git status --porcelain=v1` to an empty list, so `dirty=False` and `is_reproducible_checkout=True` whenever HEAD resolves — i.e., when the tree state could NOT be inspected, the artifact is stamped with the strongest provenance claim instead of the weakest. Result-file integrity conclusions rely on this stamp. (Normal-path behavior is correct: probed from /tmp, the current dirty repo yields '3226442+dirty' with dirty=True, 97 status entries; clean repo -> short SHA, repro=True; unborn branch -> 'no_commit+dirty'; non-repo -> 'git_unavailable'.)
confirmed.evidence: Code path: if status_proc.returncode != 0 then status_lines=[] -> dirty=bool([])=False -> line 63 `is_reproducible = has_commit and not dirty` = True. No third state distinguishes 'status unavailable' from 'status clean'.

Positive cases: cd /tmp && PYTHONPATH=/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src /opt/miniconda3/bin/python3 -c "from pathlib import Path; from rubik_optimal.source_state import capture_source_state as c; print(c(Path('/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas'))['state'])"  # 3226442+dirty
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat a failed `git status` like the git_unavailable case (dirty=None, is_reproducible_checkout=False, explicit limitation) instead of defaulting to clean.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by independent code re-read and runtime repro. In src/rubik_optimal/source_state.py line 53, a failed/None `git status --porcelain=v1` collapses to status_lines=[], so line 54 sets dirty=False and line 63 sets is_reproducible_checkout=True whenever HEAD resolves — there is no third 'status unavailable' state, unlike the git_unavailable branch (lines 30-44) which correctly sets dirty=None and repro=False. Reachability proven: with GIT_INDEX_FILE pointed at a directory, rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree and rev-parse HEAD succeed while status fails rc=128; capture_source_state on an ACTUALLY DIRTY tree under that condition returned state='b22438a', dirty=False, is_reproducible_checkout=True, limitation='' (the same tree with working git correctly returned 'b22438a+dirty', repro=False). The stamp is consumed downstream: scripts/thesis_audit.py line 430 trusts is_reproducible_checkout from stamped result files and line 482 trusts it for the current tree. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: the trigger condition is rare, the normal path behaves correctly (current repo correctly yields 3226442+dirty with 97 entries, repro=False), no evidence any saved artifact was mis-stamped, and thesis_audit's separate _git_provenance check (line 408) fails closed, partially mitigating impact. Fix is trivial: treat status failure like git_unavailable.

confirmed.title: _add_best silently masks length conflicts that would prove a source exactness flag wrong
confirmed.claim: If two certificates for the same state ever carry different lengths, at least one source artifact's "exact" flag is mathematically false; _add_best silently keeps the shorter one instead of raising or logging, so the store would hide direct evidence of unsoundness in its own inputs. Tie-breaking also keeps the first-loaded certificate, so an earlier closure-derived certificate can shadow a later genuinely-direct one, making oracle notes report certificate_derivation=symmetry for a state that was actually solved directly (provenance only, lengths unaffected).
confirmed.evidence: Code: 'if previous is None or certificate.solution_length < previous.solution_length: certificates[certificate.state] = certificate' — no conflict branch. Instrumented load of the real store found 0 conflicts among 7,562 certificates, so no current impact on any number; this is a latent safety gap, not an active bug.
confirmed.repro: Monkeypatch ExactCertificateStore._add_best to record cases where previous.solution_length != certificate.solution_length, then load the store (see /tmp/probe_store_audit.py pattern); observe 0 conflicts today and no code path that would surface one if it appeared.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In _add_best, when previous is not None and lengths differ, raise (or at minimum log a loud warning naming both artifact paths) — a length conflict between two exact certificates is a soundness alarm, not a preference choice.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code re-read and execution. (1) _add_best at src/rubik_optimal/exact_certificates.py:219-223 has no conflict branch and the module has no logging import; no call site (_load:198-203, remember_result:174-179, _add_symmetry_closure:265) detects length disagreements. Since inverse/symmetry closures preserve length and true distance is invariant under inversion and rotation conjugation, two valid same-state certificates with different lengths mathematically imply at least one source's exact flag is false — the store would hold proof of input unsoundness and silently discard it. A synthetic probe (two valid claimed-exact rows, lengths 2 and 4, same state) loads silently in either order, keeping length 2. (2) Tie-break shadowing also confirmed synthetically: a symmetry-closure cert loaded first shadows a later equal-length direct cert (derivation=symmetry, source_solver=...+symmetry_certificate_closure), and derivation is surfaced in oracle notes at src/rubik_optimal/oracle.py:2658 — provenance only, lengths unaffected. (3) Reviewer's no-current-impact evidence verified: instrumented load of the real store found 7,562 certificates, 0 length conflicts, and additionally 0 closure-shadows-direct ties, so neither failure mode is active today. Latent diagnostic/robustness gap, exactly as described; minor severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Certificate-cache-answered rows in store-listed artifacts re-enter the store as derivation="direct", laundering provenance
confirmed.claim: default_certificate_artifacts includes artifacts (e.g. portfolio_optimal_oracle_seed_2026_thesis_superflip_certificate_cache_lowload.json, universal_oracle_cli_seed_2026_thesis_h48h7_expanded_adaptive_lowload.json, universal_optimal_oracle_seed_2026_thesis_h48h7_lowload.json) that contain rows answered FROM the certificate cache. _certificate_from_row re-ingests any status=exact/verified row as a fresh derivation="direct" certificate, erasing the inheritance chain. remember_result deliberately refuses to learn from exact-certificate-cache rows (oracle.py:3324-3327), but the static artifact list has no equivalent guard. Lengths cannot be corrupted (the chain bottoms out at the original parent and every load is replay-verified), so this is provenance-only.

confirmed.suggested_fix: In _certificate_from_row, detect 'selected_backend=exact-certificate-cache' in the row's notes/selected_backend and either skip the row (the original parent is already loaded) or carry the recorded certificate_derivation forward instead of 'direct'.

votes.reasoning: Independent re-read confirms every load-bearing element of the finding. (1) Four artifacts listed in default_certificate_artifacts (src/rubik_optimal/exact_certificates.py lines 57, 65, 72, 73; the reviewer cited 71 but meant 72 — the file names are correct) contain rows with "selected_backend": "exact-certificate-cache", status="exact", verified=true: portfolio_optimal_oracle_..._superflip_certificate_cache_lowload.json, universal_optimal_oracle_..._h48h7_lowload.json, universal_oracle_cli_..._expanded_adaptive_lowload.json, universal_oracle_cli_..._superflip_probe_lowload.json. (2) _certificate_from_row (lines 279-320) gates only on status/verified, never inspects selected_backend or certificate_derivation notes tokens, and hardcodes derivation="direct" at line 319; _load has no equivalent of the guard. (3) oracle.py remember_result (lines 3324-3326) explicitly refuses selected_backend in {"exact-certificate-cache", "solved_fast_path"}, proving the design intent the static list violates. (4) The provenance-only framing is correct: the cache answer copies solution_moves/solution_length verbatim (oracle.py:2643-2644), every re-ingestion is replay-verified (verify_solution at exact_certificates.py:302), and inverse/symmetry closures preserve distance, so lengths/exactness cannot be corrupted down the chain. One mitigating fact the reviewer did not note: all cache-answered rows in the four artifacts trace to h48_resident_certification_seed_2026_thesis_h48h7_trusted.json, which loads first (line 52), and _add_best uses strict less-than so equal-length ties keep the first-inserted genuine certificate — the laundered duplicates are currently shadowed and never served. The mislabeled provenance (certificate_derivation=direct, wrong certificate_artifact/source_solver in oracle notes at oracle.py:2655-2661, and derivation filters in scripts/run_certificate_cache_*_closure.py and scripts/thesis_audit.py:2566-2607) would only surface if list order changes, the source artifact disappears (missing artifacts are silently skipped at exact_certificates.py:186-187), or custom artifact_paths are used. Real but latent and provenance-only; "minor" is the right severity.

confirmed.title: Symmetry-closure script hardcodes a 'proven' claim and writes a misleading per-derivation runtime column

confirmed.title: Headline superflip certification 51.813928s is cache-state dependent; an uncited sibling artifact shows the same state timing out at the same 90s budget
confirmed.claim: The 51.813928s superflip run was a checked-table run, where the per-call nissy_checkdata scan of the full 3.8 GB table doubles as a page-cache warm-up before the search. The sibling artifact h48_oracle_certification_seed_2026_thesis_trusted.json (same script, same 90s timeout, trusted mode so no warm-up scan) has passed=false with superflip timing out at 90.061831s; a second trusted run timed out at 240s (auto_min artifact). Saved single-call superflip results therefore span 51.8s to 158.4s plus two timeouts depending on cache state. The thesis quotes the favorable checked run as 'under the 90s target' and does discuss the 91.6s and 158.4s runs and the auto-min failure, but never mentions the plain trusted@90 failure sitting in results/processed, and does not state that the 51.8s figure benefits from the integrity scan acting as a preload.
confirmed.evidence: h48_oracle_certification_seed_2026_thesis.json: superflip exact 20 in 51.813928s (timeout 90, checked mode, artifact predates the trusted_table key). h48_oracle_certification_seed_2026_thesis_trusted.json: passed=false, errors include 'superflip_distance_20: expected exact verified result, got timeout' at 90.061831s. h48_oracle_certification_seed_2026_thesis_auto_min.json: passed=false, superflip timeout at 240.045481s. Grep of all thesis chapters finds no reference to 90.016105/90.061831.
confirmed.repro: python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/results/processed/h48_oracle_certification_seed_2026_thesis_trusted.json')); print(d['passed'], d['errors'])" and grep -rn '90.0618' /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/thesis/chapters/.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add one sentence at 05_experiments.tex:185 noting that in the checked configuration the integrity scan also pages the table into memory before the search, and that a trusted-mode run at the same 90s budget timed out (citing the artifact), so the 51.8s figure assumes a warmed table.

votes.reasoning: Every checkable element of the finding verified. (1) Mechanism: h48_backend.c mmaps the 3.79 GB table lazily (line 319) and, absent --skip-table-check, runs nissy_checkdata before search (line 602); checkdata_h48 -> getdistribution_h48 (nissy_core src/solvers/h48/distribution_h48.h:25-33) reads every byte of the pruning table, so in checked mode every single-call solve performs a full sequential scan that faults the entire table into the page cache before nissy_solve. The certification script spawns a fresh process per case, so earlier rows' scans also warm the cache for the superflip row. (2) Artifacts: the headline checked artifact (passed=true, superflip 51.813928s @ 90s timeout, no trusted_table key) and the uncited sibling h48_oracle_certification_seed_2026_thesis_trusted.json (identical config: 8 threads, h48h7, same table, same 90s timeout/target; passed=false, superflip timeout 90.061831s, depth_25 timeout 90.016105s) both exist with exactly the claimed values, plus 91.629342s (trusted@300), 158.440286s (trusted_preload@180), and 240s auto_min timeouts. The depth_25 contrast (0.93s backend solve checked vs 90s timeout trusted) is near-conclusive evidence of cache-state dependence. (3) Thesis: 05_experiments.tex:185 quotes 51.813928s as under the 90s target; only the checked and trusted_preload tables are \input; the generated thesis/tables/h48_oracle_certification_trusted.tex (with the 90.016/90.061 timeouts) is never included and no chapter references those numbers or the passed=false trusted@90 run; the 91.6s/158.4s runs and the auto-min failure are discussed, exactly as the finding says. 04_implementation.tex:96-98 shows awareness of per-call checkdata and cold page faults but never discloses that the headline figure benefits from the scan-as-preload or that the same 90s budget failed in trusted mode. Severity: the exactness/distance-20 result is unaffected (independently proven), and the thesis discloses most timing variance, so this is a selective-reporting/presentation gap — minor is right, though at the upper edge since the contradicting passed=false artifact sits uncited in results/processed.

confirmed.title: All speedups are single ordered runs (n=1) quoted at 6-decimal precision, and the scripts' pass-gates require minimum speedups
confirmed.claim: Each arm total is measured exactly once per script invocation (the 'repetitions' are the workload, not statistical repeats of the comparison), with no variance, no cache-state control, and a fixed arm order. The thesis quotes these single-run ratios (11.56x, 38.198x, 40.16x, 189.894x) as the result. Additionally, the scripts return exit code 1 unless the speedup exceeds a hard-coded threshold (2.0x for trusted-table, 1.0x for batch/resident), so retained 'passed' artifacts are by construction runs where the speedup materialized. The only variability caveat is a general sentence in 06_validation.tex:39 (hardware, cache warming, timeouts), not at the quote sites.
confirmed.evidence: benchmark_h48_trusted_table.py:135-139: payload['passed'] = (... and (payload['trusted_speedup'] or 0) >= 2.0). No repeat/median/min logic exists in any of the three benchmark scripts; time.perf_counter() brackets each arm exactly once.
confirmed.repro: Read the three 'passed' computations at the cited lines; confirm no loop over the arm pair exists in any script.
confirmed.suggested_fix: State in the thesis that each speedup is a single recorded run, or rerun each benchmark 3-5 times and quote median with range; remove the speedup floor from 'passed' or document it as a regression gate rather than a measurement criterion.

votes.reasoning: Code re-read confirms every structural claim. (1) Each arm comparison is measured exactly once per script invocation: benchmark_h48_trusted_table.py brackets each arm with a single perf_counter pair around a workload loop (_run_mode lines 54-67; arms invoked once each at lines 91-112); benchmark_h48_batch_overhead.py (lines 96-128) and benchmark_h48_resident_oracle.py (lines 75-105) are identical in structure. No median/min/stdev/repeat logic exists in any of the three scripts, and no wrapper aggregates multiple runs (thesis_audit.py only lists the scripts as required files). (2) Arm order is fixed with the cold/slow arm first (checked->trusted, sequential->batch, separate->resident), so the fast arm always runs with a warmed OS page cache for the multi-GB table. (3) Pass-gates confirmed at the cited lines: trusted_table.py:135-139 requires trusted_speedup >= 2.0; batch_overhead.py:159-163 and resident_oracle.py:142-146 require >= 1.0; all three scripts exit 1 unless passed (lines 145, 169, 152), so retained passed=true artifacts are selected on success. (4) The thesis quotes the single-run ratios verbatim at 6-decimal-second precision: 05_experiments.tex:215 (11.56x), :229 (189.894x), :244 (38.198x), :303 (40.16x); all values match the saved JSON artifacts in results/processed/ exactly. One small inaccuracy in the finding: the general variability caveat is at 05_experiments.tex:39, not 06_validation.tex:39, but its substance (general sentence about hardware/cache warming/timeouts, not at the quote sites) is correct; the quote sites do carry scope caveats (e.g. 'not worst-case search speedup') but no run-variance caveat. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: the 11x-190x effects come from mechanistically deterministic overhead elimination (per-process table verification / process spawn), so n=1 cannot plausibly flip the qualitative conclusion, gate thresholds sit far below observed values, and these are engineering-overhead claims rather than exactness/optimality claims. Recommended fix is cheap: repeat the comparison a few times, quote median plus range at coarser precision, and add a one-line n=1 caveat at the quote sites.

confirmed.title: Trusted mode never verifies table bytes at solve time; optimality of 'exact verified' trusted rows rests on the offline checksum run, and 04_implementation slightly overstates the per-call guarantee
confirmed.claim: What --trusted-table skips is the native nissy_checkdata full scan (h48_backend.c:602). The Python-side replacement checks only canonical path, metadata fields, file size, and that the recorded checksum is 64 hex chars — it never hashes the table (docstring: 'intentionally avoids recomputing the full SHA-256'). The independent verifier replays returned solutions, which proves only the upper bound; minimality is inherited from the pruning table's correctness. So a corrupted same-size table would pass trusted validation and could yield non-optimal rows still labeled 'exact, verified'. The chain is closed only by scripts/verify_results.py:327 (full SHA-256 vs generation metadata) run separately. 04_implementation.tex:98 says trusted mode applies to tables 'ήδη παραχθεί και ταυτοποιηθεί από metadata/checksum', which a reader can take as per-call checksum identification; the thesis never states explicitly that the per-call check is metadata-only and the byte-level check is offline.
confirmed.evidence: tables/h48.py:331-332 — the only checksum-related per-call check is len(checksum) != 64 / hex-character validation; no sha256_file call in validate_trusted_h48_table. verify_results.py:327: 'if h48.get("checksum_sha256") != sha256_file(table_path)'. h48_backend.c:602: nissy_checkdata gated on !skip_table_check.
confirmed.repro: Read tables/h48.py:279-333 and confirm absence of any file-content hashing; then verify_results.py:314-334 for where the real hash happens.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add one sentence at 04_implementation.tex:98 (and where the 189.894x is quoted): trusted mode validates metadata identity only; byte-level integrity is established once by scripts/verify_results.py SHA-256 against generation metadata, and exactness of trusted rows is conditional on that offline verification.

votes.reasoning: Every factual element verified by independent code reading. (1) src/rubik_optimal/tables/h48.py:279-333 validate_trusted_h48_table performs no file hashing; the only checksum check is shape validation (64 hex chars) at lines 331-332, and the docstring admits it avoids recomputing SHA-256. (2) The normal solve path (oracle.py:820-848; FastOptimalOracleConfig.trusted_table defaults True at oracle.py:110) uses this metadata-only check and passes --skip-table-check; the full-hash variant validate_trusted_h48_table_checksum (h48.py:336, sha256_file at 469) is wired only into bundle/install/cloud-worker preflight scripts, not the solve path. (3) native/h48_backend/h48_backend.c:602 gates nissy_checkdata on !skip_table_check, and records table_check="skipped" in trusted mode. (4) Per-solve verification (h48_native.py:208,225) replays the solution via verify_solution — upper bound only; minimality rests on pruning-table integrity, so a same-size corrupted table with intact metadata would pass trusted validation and could yield a non-minimal row still labeled exact/verified. (5) The byte-level chain is closed only by scripts/verify_results.py:327-328 (full sha256_file vs generation metadata), run offline. (6) thesis/chapters/04_implementation.tex:98 wording confirmed; it says trusted mode skips the per-call nissy_checkdata scan for tables "ήδη παραχθεί και ταυτοποιηθεί από metadata/checksum" but never states the per-call check is metadata-only with the SHA-256 comparison performed offline — a reader can plausibly infer per-call checksum identification. Mitigations (table_check=skipped disclosed in artifacts, offline verifier, cloud-worker full-checksum preflight) soften but do not contradict the finding, which already credits them. Severity minor is right: intentional documented tradeoff, no reported numbers change while verify_results.py passes; remedy is a one-sentence thesis clarification.

confirmed.title: run_h48_oracle_cli.py wrapper timeout bounds the whole batch with a single-case budget
confirmed.claim: The wrapper runs the 4-case CLI batch under subprocess timeout=args.timeout + 30.0, while each non-solved case may legitimately search up to args.timeout (per-case --timeout is forwarded to the CLI). Two or more slow cases would exceed the wrapper budget, raise TimeoutExpired uncaught, and abort the evidence run without an artifact. By contrast solve_h48_native_batch correctly multiplies the grace budget by the number of pending cases (h48_native.py:2347-2349). Latent only: the recorded runs finished in 5.9s/2.1s, far under budget, so no recorded number is affected.
confirmed.evidence: run_h48_oracle_cli.py:114 'timeout=args.timeout + 30.0' wrapping a 4-input batch whose CLI receives '--timeout str(args.timeout)' per case (lines 97-98); no try/except around subprocess.run for TimeoutExpired.
confirmed.repro: Read scripts/run_h48_oracle_cli.py:88-115 and compare with src/rubik_optimal/solvers/h48_native.py:2347-2349 (process_timeout *= len(pending)).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use timeout=(args.timeout * len(cases)) + 30.0, mirroring the batch wrapper's per-case budget multiplication.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed by direct code reading at every level of the stack. (1) scripts/run_h48_oracle_cli.py:107-115 wraps the whole 4-case batch in subprocess.run(timeout=args.timeout + 30.0) with no try/except for subprocess.TimeoutExpired; the artifact write (write_json, line 157) is only reached after subprocess.run returns, so a timeout aborts the evidence run with an uncaught traceback and no JSON/TeX artifact. (2) Lines 97-98 forward --timeout per the CLI's per-case semantics: the CLI oracle default path (src/rubik_optimal/cli.py:1628-1640, no --stream/--universal/--rubikoptimal) calls solve_h48_native_batch(timeout_seconds=args.timeout), which passes --search-timeout-ms to the native binary, and the native backend (native/h48_backend/h48_backend.c run_solve_batch lines 731-757) resets the deadline inside solve_loaded_cube for each stdin line (lines 498-501) — so the search budget is genuinely per case. (3) The codebase's own convention confirms the mismatch: h48_native.py:2347-2349 computes process_timeout = _process_timeout_with_grace(timeout_seconds) * len(pending); for the default timeout=60 and 3 pending cases (the solved case is pre-resolved at h48_native.py:2257-2275 and never hits the backend) the inner budget is ~198s while the wrapper kills at 90s. Two near-timeout cases would legitimately exceed the wrapper budget. (4) Latent-only confirmed: recorded artifacts results/processed/h48_oracle_cli_seed_2026_thesis.json and ..._trusted.json show wrapper_wall_seconds of 5.92s and 2.13s vs the 90s budget, both passed=true, so no thesis number is affected. Severity "minor" is right: failure mode is a loud crash in a harness script on hypothetical slow inputs, not silently wrong or unsound data.

confirmed.title: Chapter 04 describes representative-solution recovery as 'BFS with parents'; the code stores full per-node paths via an independent per-query BFS, and only one shallow test cross-checks solver length against an oracle
confirmed.claim: The text says 'Για μεμονωμένες αντιπροσωπευτικές περιπτώσεις χρησιμοποιείται BFS με γονείς ώστε να ανακτηθεί συγκεκριμένη βέλτιστη λύση'. The actual implementation (pocket_optimal_solution, src/rubik_optimal/pocket/optimal.py:195-230) is a forward BFS from the scrambled state that stores a complete move-list per queue entry — no parent pointers, and it does not descend the exhaustive distance table at all. The optimality of the printed table-of-representatives therefore rests on the per-query BFS (with same-face pruning and a coord-keyed visited set), not on the exhaustive table; the only in-repo cross-check of solver length against an oracle is the single depth-3 case in tests/test_pocket_cube.py:37-49.
confirmed.evidence: optimal.py:206 'queue: list[tuple[int, list[str], str | None]] = [(start_coord, [], None)]' and :225 'next_path = path + [move]' — path lists, not parents. I verified the construction is nonetheless exact: the same-face pruning preserves distances (consecutive same-face moves merge/cancel, and the predecessor's incoming move is never on the pruned face), and empirically all five representative solutions (lengths 1,2,3,4,6) replay to solved with lengths equal to distances from an independent cubie-level exhaustive BFS to depth 6 that bypasses all pocket coordinate/table code.
confirmed.repro: Run the probe: PYTHONPATH=/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src /opt/miniconda3/bin/python3 /tmp/pocket_probe2.py (cubie-level BFS over CubeState corners only; prints bfs_dist == solution_length == artifact length for all 5 reps, all solve, all state_coords match). Read optimal.py:195-230 to confirm path-list BFS, no parent map and no distance-table descent.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Reword 04_implementation.tex:133 to 'BFS που κρατά το μονοπάτι κάθε κόμβου' (or similar), and optionally add a test asserting pocket_optimal_solution length == compute_pocket_distribution distance for a few random states at depths 8-11 so representative optimality is machine-checked against the exhaustive table, not just argued.

votes.reasoning: Re-read confirms every element. (1) thesis/chapters/04_implementation.tex:133 explicitly says representative solutions are recovered by 'BFS με γονείς' (BFS with parents), contrasting with the distances-only full BFS. (2) The actual implementation, pocket_optimal_solution at src/rubik_optimal/pocket/optimal.py:195-230, is a forward BFS storing a complete move list per queue entry (line 206 tuple (coord, path, previous); line 225 next_path = path + [move]); there is no parent map, and it never consults the exhaustive distance table (compute_pocket_distribution discards per-state distances anyway). (3) The printed representatives table (thesis/chapters/05_experiments.tex:490 inputs tables/pocket_cube_representatives.tex) is produced by scripts/generate_pocket_cube.py:41-58 via solve_pocket_cube_optimal (src/rubik_optimal/solvers/pocket_cube.py:18-30, status 'exact'), so its optimality rests solely on this per-query BFS. (4) The only length-vs-oracle cross-check is the depth-3 case in tests/test_pocket_cube.py:37-49; scripts/verify_results.py:212-214 checks only status flags. Attempted refutations failed: 'BFS with parents' could be read loosely, but the sentence draws an explicit parents-vs-no-parents contrast and the mechanism differs; and the per-query BFS is in fact exact (I proved the same-face pruning + visited-set construction preserves distances, and an independent cubie-level BFS oracle I wrote confirms all five artifact lengths 1,2,3,4,6 are optimal, solutions replay to solved, coords match), which means no published number is wrong — capping severity at minor, not eliminating the finding. Minor is the right rating: a text/implementation mechanism mismatch in the implementation chapter plus thin oracle coverage for 'exact'-labeled results, easily fixed by rewording line 133 (e.g. 'BFS που κρατά τη διαδρομή ανά κόμβο') and/or adding a test cross-checking representative lengths against an independent BFS.

votes.reasoning: Direct re-read confirms the defect exactly as described: in /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/scripts/generate_pocket_cube.py, --seed is parsed at line 28 (default 2026), and args.seed appears only at line 63 (JSON payload) and lines 70-71 (artifact filenames). No random module is imported and no RNG exists anywhere in the script or in src/rubik_optimal/pocket/* or src/rubik_optimal/solvers/pocket_cube.py (verified by grep). The pipeline is fully deterministic: exhaustive BFS distribution over all 3,674,160 pocket states, a hardcoded list of 5 representative sequences, and a deterministic optimal solver. The artifacts results/processed/pocket_cube_summary_seed_2026_{quick,thesis}.json exist with "seed": 2026 in the payload, so the filename/payload do imply seeded randomness that does not exist. One nuance softens the framing but does not refute the finding: this is a repo-wide pattern, not unique to the pocket script — e.g. scripts/generate_corner_pdb.py (lines 79, 102, 125) likewise bakes an unused --seed into the deterministic corner-PDB filename and metadata, so "seed_2026" partly functions as a campaign/provenance tag for deterministic artifacts too. That means the answer to a supervisor would be "it is a provenance tag" rather than an isolated oddity, but the cosmetic confusion is real. Minor severity is appropriate: no number, optimality claim, or result is affected.

confirmed.title: Solver facade labels a hypothetical failed-verification outcome as 'timeout'
confirmed.claim: status is computed as 'exact' if verified else 'timeout', where verified = (solution is not None and replay solves). If the BFS ever returned a sequence that fails replay verification (i.e., a genuine bug), the result would be reported as 'timeout' rather than an error — a mislabel under the repo's own academic-integrity taxonomy that distinguishes timeouts from incorrect output. In practice this branch is unreachable for valid states (max_depth default 14 >= the proven maximum distance 11, so a verified solution always exists), so impact is nil today.
confirmed.evidence: Lines 19/30: 'verified = solution is not None and state.apply_sequence(solution).is_solved()' ... 'status="exact" if verified else "timeout"'. There is no third status for found-but-unverified.
confirmed.repro: Read src/rubik_optimal/solvers/pocket_cube.py:12-33; note pocket_optimal_solution can only return None (depth bound) or a replayable path, and POCKET_GODS_NUMBER == 11 < 14, so the 'timeout' branch is dead for valid inputs.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Distinguish the cases: status='timeout' only when solution is None; raise or status='error' if a solution exists but fails verification.

votes.reasoning: Direct re-read confirms the code: src/rubik_optimal/solvers/pocket_cube.py:19 computes verified = (solution is not None and replay solves) and line 30 maps everything non-verified to status="timeout" with no third status. The found-but-unverified path exists in code (BFS solves via coordinate tables in pocket/optimal.py:195-230; verification replays via cubie-level apply_sequence, so a table/cubie divergence would land there) and would be mislabeled "timeout" instead of "failed". This contradicts the repo's own taxonomy: benchmark.py:444 enumerates a distinct "failed" status, and sibling solvers (rubikoptimal_external.py:643/846, thistlethwaite.py:259) use status="exact" if verification.ok else "failed" for exactly this condition. The reviewer's impact assessment is also accurate: the branch is dead for valid inputs (POCKET_GODS_NUMBER=11 < default max_depth=14, table-vs-cubie cross-check and full-distribution regression tests guard the only failure mechanism), the sole consumer (scripts/generate_pocket_cube.py:45) uses the default depth, and the mislabel goes in the safe direction (never reports "exact" for unverified output, is_verified stays False), so there is no academic-integrity unsoundness. Severity "minor" is correct: a one-line labeling fix in a currently unreachable branch with no effect on reported results.

questions.title: Strength-measurement rows label scramble length as state depth ("rand_d10..rand_d20")
questions.claim: Rows in the saved artifact are labeled rand_d10..rand_d20 where N is the scramble length, which is only an upper bound on the true distance (the repo's own WORSTCASE doc section 5.4 warns about exactly this conflation). For an h-gain measurement this is harmless, and my oracle probe confirmed scramble lengths 8-14 frequently overstate true depth (e.g. a length-9 scramble at d=8, length-13 at d=12). If the thesis ever quotes these rows as "depth-N states" it would overstate the evidence; currently only the design doc references the artifact, so this needs an author decision on wording, not a code change.
questions.evidence: Artifact rows {"state": "rand_d20", ...} are generated from CubeState.from_sequence(_scramble(rng, depth)) with no distance certification; my probe: rand_d9 -> native d=8, rand_d13 -> native d=12 (same construction, different seed).
questions.repro: Read scripts/measure_seven_edge_strength.py:117-124; compare with the oracle probe pattern: PYTHONPATH=src python3 /tmp/probe_admissibility.py (rand_d9 row shows d=8).
questions.suggested_fix: Rename labels to rand_len{N} (or annotate "scramble length, distance upper bound") in the script and artifact before any thesis text cites them as depths.

questions.title: Native h48 backend labels a solution 'exact' even when the deadline poll fired (relies on undocumented nissy iterative-deepening invariant)
questions.claim: exact_solution_found = result > 0 && solution[0] != '\0' does not require !timed_out_by_poll && !search_deadline_expired (those flags only guard the negative-search lower-bound claim). A solution returned by an interrupted nissy_solve call is therefore still reported status='exact'. This is sound only under the invariant that nissy-core's solver searches depth thresholds strictly in increasing order and fully completes each threshold before the next, so any returned solution sits at the first feasible depth — an invariant of the vendored third-party code that is neither asserted nor tested locally, and could silently break on a nissy-core update.
questions.evidence: h48_backend.c:531-533: 'exact_solution_found = result > 0 && solution[0] != ‘\0’; completed_negative_search = result == 0 && !timed_out_by_poll && !search_deadline_expired;' — the timeout flags are consulted only for completed_negative_search, not for the exact branch at line 536.
questions.repro: Read /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/native/h48_backend/h48_backend.c lines 498-541; compare which flags gate the 'exact' vs 'lower_bound' JSON status. Downstream Python (_result_from_payload in solvers/h48_native.py:194-247) passes the backend status through after replay verification, which proves only validity, not minimality.
questions.suggested_fix: Conservatively downgrade to status='timeout' when timed_out_by_poll || search_deadline_expired even if a solution string is present (or emit it as a 'non_exact' upper bound), or document and regression-test the nissy iterative-deepening invariant the exact label depends on.

questions.title: Exactness for non-shallow states is inherited from third-party optimal solvers; local re-verification proves validity only
questions.claim: For all backend paths except BFS (depth<=5), own-PDB IDA*, completed bounded proofs, and upper==lower certificates, status='exact' means: an external optimal solver (vendored nissy-core h48 with -O 0 / iterative deepening, external nissy 2.x 'solve optimal -o', Kociemba's RubikOptimal '(Nf*)') asserted minimality, and the repo verified only that the solution is VALID for the input state. Minimality is never independently re-derived. This is a defensible and disclosed design (notes carry exactness_policy strings; convention-mismatch risks are fully covered because a wrong state translation would make the returned solution fail local replay), but the supervisor may challenge whether G2's 'exact recognition' is proved by the student's implementation or delegated. The genuinely self-contained proof rules should be clearly identified in the thesis: BFS exhaustion, own IDA*+PDB, h48 upper-bound proofs via completed negative search (h48_backend.c proved_lower_bound = max_depth+1 only when !timed_out), and upper==lower certificates.
questions.evidence: oracle.py:991-1009 marks nissy output 'exact' purely on verification.ok of replay (validity); nissy_external.py:1268 same for nissy-core direct; rubikoptimal_external.py _result_from_row same. Live probes show these agree with exhaustive BFS at depths 4-6, and all 2,521 saved exact rows replay correctly, but no local minimality proof exists for the deep rows except the upper-lower/bounded-proof subsets.
questions.repro: grep -n 'status="exact" if verification.ok' /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/solvers/nissy_external.py /Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src/rubik_optimal/oracle.py — then confirm no surrounding code compares against an independent lower bound on those paths.
questions.suggested_fix: No code change strictly required; in the thesis, classify each reported exact result by proof rule (own exhaustive search / upper==lower certificate / external-optimal-solver + local validity replay) so the exactness provenance survives supervisor scrutiny. Optionally add an independent h48 lower-bound cross-check (lower <= returned length is a cheap sanity assertion) to every external-exact path.

questions.title: Chapter wording 'independent verification' overstates the verifier's independence for the native paths; solved-input early return skips replay entirely
questions.claim: The chapter claims each row gets 'ανεξάρτητη επαλήθευση της λύσης'. For the adapter rows (both deep cases in the published table) this is genuinely independent: an external package's solution is replayed on the student's own cube model. But for the native scoped paths, verify_solution replays the moves on the same CubeState move engine the solver itself searches with, so a hypothetical move-engine bug would be invisible to verification; and the solved-input early return in solve_kociemba_native_scoped sets is_verified=True without calling verify_solution at all (trivially correct for an empty solution, but it means the 'solved' table row's verified flag never exercised the replay path). Whether 'independent' should be qualified to 'independent of the solver's search/tables' is a wording decision for the author/supervisor.
questions.evidence: kociemba.py lines 705-719: 'if cube.is_solved(): return SolverResult(..., is_verified=True, ...)' with no verify_solution call; verify.py line 26 'final = initial.apply_sequence(moves)' uses the same CubeState.apply_move engine as the solvers; chapter line 42 text: 'Για κάθε γραμμή εκτελείται validation, αναγνώριση απόστασης ή κάτω φραγής, αυτόματη πρακτική επίλυση και ανεξάρτητη επαλήθευση της λύσης.'
questions.repro: Read src/rubik_optimal/verify.py lines 18-29 and src/rubik_optimal/solvers/kociemba.py lines 705-719, then thesis/chapters/05_experiments.tex line 42.
questions.suggested_fix: Either qualify the thesis wording (verification is an independent REPLAY of the solution on the cube model, not an independent model implementation), or strengthen it cheaply: have verify_solution for native-path results also check the facelet-level reconstruction, and call verify_solution even in the solved-input early return.

questions.title: Verified positive: the new superflip 'exactly 20' contribution in the introduction diff is fully backed by saved native artifacts
questions.claim: Not a defect — recorded so the supervisor-facing audit trail is explicit. The uncommitted diff adds the claim of a native (no H48/Nissy) proof that superflip distance is exactly 20 via a 16-symmetry FlipUDSlice phase-1 table, three-axis Reid bound, and exhaustive single-bound IDA* excluding all solutions of length <=19. Every element checks out, including one subtlety worth the author double-documenting in ch. 05/06: the length<=19 exclusion run restricted root moves to {U, U2} (--root-move-mask), which is exact-safe ONLY because the superflip's symmetry stabilizer is the full 48-element group (superflip is central); the artifact records this policy, and the thesis text should state the symmetry-reduction argument wherever the proof is presented, since a skeptical examiner will ask why 2 root moves suffice out of 18.

questions.title: Closure uses only the 24 proper rotations — reflections verified safe but unused; closure orbit otherwise complete
questions.claim: _add_symmetry_closure iterates CUBE_ROTATIONS (24) rather than CUBE_SYMMETRIES (48). My probes show the reflection move maps are correct (reflected clockwise quarter turns map to counterclockwise; conjugation homomorphism holds for all 48), and d(sigma(s))=d(s) holds for reflections too, so including them would be sound and would roughly double zero-search coverage. Conversely, the implemented one-level closure (rotations of s plus rotations of s^-1) IS the complete orbit under the group generated by rotations and inversion, since sigma(s)^-1 = sigma(s^-1) — no fixpoint iteration is missing. This is a deliberate-looking coverage choice the author/supervisor should confirm (e.g. whether 'rotational symmetry closure' wording in artifacts is meant to exclude reflections intentionally).
questions.evidence: Probe output: 24 reflections, 0 quarter-turn sign mismatches, 0 homomorphism failures over 30 random state/sequence trials x 48 symmetries; exact_certificates.py:254 'for rotation in CUBE_ROTATIONS:'. Store counts (3634 symmetry vs 160x23=3680 upper bound) confirm only rotations are applied, with stabilizer collisions (e.g. superflip maps to itself under all rotations).
questions.repro: PYTHONPATH=/Users/alextoska/Desktop/sgarbas/src /opt/miniconda3/bin/python3 /tmp/probe_symmetry_math.py (or re-create: check sym.move_map[face] endswith "'" for the 24 improper CUBE_SYMMETRIES and the conjugation identity on random cubes).
questions.suggested_fix: If extra coverage is wanted, switch the closure loop to CUBE_SYMMETRIES (the existing per-derivation verify_solution guard already protects validity); otherwise document that reflections are intentionally excluded.

questions.title: Discussion chapter under-claims: the fixed-DBL U/R/F model is exactly the standard rotation-quotient convention, so the computed 11 IS the published God's Number, not merely 'coinciding'
questions.claim: The text says the distribution 'δεν είναι δήλωση για κάθε possible physical orientation του 2x2x2 με όλες τις συμμετρίες. Είναι δήλωση για το συγκεκριμένο fixed-reference μοντέλο και το συγκεκριμένο σύνολο κινήσεων U,R,F'. This is safe but weaker than what is true: on a 2x2 every D/L/B face turn equals a U/R/F turn composed with a whole-cube rotation, so the fixed-DBL quotient with 9 generators is isometric to the physical 2x2x2 under all 18 HTM moves modulo the 24 rotations — exactly the convention under which the literature reports 3,674,160 states, max 11, 2,644 antipodes. The hedged wording may itself draw the supervisor question 'is your 11 the published 11 or a model-specific number?'
questions.evidence: The computed distribution {0:1, 1:9, 2:54, 3:321, 4:1847, 5:9992, 6:50136, 7:227536, 8:870072, 9:1887748, 10:623800, 11:2644} (results/processed/pocket_cube_summary_seed_2026_thesis.json, reproduced byte-identically by rerun) equals the published 2x2x2 HTM table entry for entry. The equivalence argument is one paragraph: rotations act simply transitively on the 24 placements of the DBL cubie, so each rotation class has a unique fixed-DBL representative, and conjugating any D/L/B generator by the normalizing rotation yields a U/R/F generator, preserving word length.
questions.repro: Compare the saved distribution to the standard published 2x2x2 HTM table; for the group argument, verify in the repo model that for each face X in {D,L,B}, the corner action of X equals (rotation) o (opposite-face move) o (rotation^-1) restricted to corners — or simply note the byte-identical match with the literature values.
questions.suggested_fix: Author/supervisor decision: either keep the conservative scoping, or add a short remark (with the new citation from finding 1) that the fixed-reference model is the standard quotient convention, making the comparison with the published God's Number 11 exact rather than coincidental.

refuted.title: g1_preserving_symmetries() selects the 16-symmetry group by sampling 3 fixed G1 states, not structurally
refuted.refutation: The reviewer read the code correctly (symmetry.py:234-243 does filter the 48 symmetries by mapping 3 fixed G1 samples into G1), and the function's output is exactly the structural 16-element UD-axis stabilizer — both verified by running the repro. But the finding's defect mechanism is refuted on analysis. (1) The filter is provably one-sided: every UD-axis-preserving symmetry conjugates the G1 generator set into itself, so all 16 correct symmetries pass for ANY samples taken from G1 (and the hard-coded samples use only G1 generators; probe: 0 violations over 16 syms x 500 random G1 states). Hence any wrong selection must be a strict superset with count > 16 — the hypothesized 'wrong-but-16-element selection' is mathematically impossible. (2) Consequently the gates DO necessarily catch every possible error: the sole producer of the FlipUDSlice reduction, build_reduction() at scripts/generate_phase1_sym_tables.py:175-177, raises RuntimeError unless count==16 and identity-first, so 'silently corrupt the FlipUDSlice reduction' cannot occur — any bad selection aborts table generation loudly. tests/test_symmetry.py:73-74 additionally pins count==16 and the exact superflip root representatives. (3) 'Empirical-by-luck' overstates fragility: each single non-trivial sample alone already reduces the 48 to exactly the structural 16 (probe confirmed), the function takes no inputs and is a deterministic module-level constant. The residual truth — that a structural face_map['U'] in ('U','D') filter would be more self-evidently correct than the sample filter — is a pure style/clarity preference about provably equivalent code, below the reporting bar for a capped pre-submission audit where the reviewer's own evidence concedes correctness. Worth at most a one-line mention folded into a summary, not a standalone finding.
